Body:
```markdown
# [Feature] Add MiMo-V2.5 to Xiaomi catalog + automatic multimodal routing when DeepSeek V4-Pro is primary model

# [Feature] Add MiMo-V2.5 to Xiaomi catalog + automatic multimodal routing when DeepSeek V4-Pro is primary model

## Summary

Two related asks in one issue:

1. **Add `xiaomi/mimo-v2.5` to the built-in Xiaomi provider catalog** — it was released on April 22 2026 and supersedes `mimo-v2-omni` across every multimodal benchmark.
2. **Auto-route to a multimodal model when the primary model is text-only and the incoming message contains image / video / audio attachments** — so users running DeepSeek V4-Pro as their default never have to manually switch models.

---

## Why MiMo-V2.5 instead of MiMo-V2-Omni

| Property | `mimo-v2-omni` | `mimo-v2.5` |
|---|---|---|
| Parameters | — | 310B total / 15B active (sparse MoE) |
| Modalities | text, image | text, image, video, audio |
| Context window | 262,144 | 1,048,576 |
| Max output | 32,000 | 131,072 |
| Reasoning | ✅ | ✅ |
| Input price | — | $0.40 / 1M tokens |
| Output price | — | $2.00 / 1M tokens |
| API model id | `mimo-v2-omni` | `mimo-v2.5` |
| Base URL | `https://api.xiaomimimo.com/v1` | same |

MiMo-V2.5 trains image, video, and audio encoders jointly from the start — not adapter-bolted-on. It also beats MiMo-V2-Omni on Video-MME (87.7) and MMMU-Pro (77.9), and runs at roughly half the cost of MiMo-V2-Pro.

---

## Requested catalog entry

Add to `src/providers/xiaomi/catalog.ts` (or equivalent):

```ts
{
 id: "mimo-v2.5",
 name: "Xiaomi MiMo V2.5",
 reasoning: true,
 input: ["text", "image", "video", "audio"],
 cost: { input: 0.40, output: 2.00, cacheRead: 0.04, cacheWrite: 0 },
 contextWindow: 1_048_576,
 maxTokens: 131_072,
},
```

> The existing `mimo-v2-omni` entry should be kept for backward compat but marked as superseded in docs.

---

## Requested behavior: automatic multimodal routing

### Problem

When a user sets `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro` (or any text-only model) as primary:

```jsonc
// openclaw.config.json
{
 "agents": {
 "defaults": {
 "model": { "primary": "deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro" }
 }
 }
}
```

…and then attaches an image, video, or audio file to a message, OpenClaw currently either errors out or strips the attachment silently.

### Proposed behavior

OpenClaw should inspect the `input` capability array of the resolved primary model before dispatching. If the message contains attachment types the primary model does not declare support for, it should automatically re-route that single turn to a configured **multimodal fallback**, then resume the primary model on subsequent text-only turns.

Suggested config surface:

```jsonc
{
 "agents": {
 "defaults": {
 "model": {
 "primary": "deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro",
 "multimodalFallback": "xiaomi/mimo-v2.5"
 }
 }
 }
}
```

### Routing rules

| Primary model supports | Message contains | Action |
|---|---|---|
| `["text"]` | text only | dispatch to primary ✅ |
| `["text"]` | image / video / audio | re-route turn to `multimodalFallback` |
| `["text", "image"]` | image | dispatch to primary ✅ |
| `["text", "image"]` | video or audio | re-route turn to `multimodalFallback` |
| `["text", "image", "video", "audio"]` | anything | dispatch to primary ✅ |

The multimodal fallback response should be injected into the conversation history as an assistant message attributed to the primary model alias, so the context is seamless.

### Why not just use `failover`?

Failover fires on error. This needs to fire *before* the request is sent, based on input type inspection — a different code path.

---

## Minimal reproducible config (for testing)

```jsonc
{
 "env": {
 "DEEPSEEK_API_KEY": "sk-...",
 "XIAOMI_API_KEY": "xm-..."
 },
 "agents": {
 "defaults": {
 "model": {
 "primary": "deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro",
 "multimodalFallback": "xiaomi/mimo-v2.5"
 }
 }
 },
 "models": {
 "mode": "merge",
 "providers": {
 "deepseek": {
 "baseUrl": "https://api.deepseek.com/v1",
 "api": "openai-completions",
 "apiKey": "***"
 },
 "xiaomi": {
 "baseUrl": "https://api.xiaomimimo.com/v1",
 "api": "openai-completions",
 "apiKey": "***",
 "models": [
 {
 "id": "mimo-v2.5",
 "name": "Xiaomi MiMo V2.5",
 "reasoning": true,
 "input": ["text", "image", "video", "audio"],
 "cost": { "input": 0.40, "output": 2.00, "cacheRead": 0.04, "cacheWrite": 0 },
 "contextWindow": 1048576,
 "maxTokens": 131072
 }
 ]
 }
 }
 }
}
```

Expected behavior when a user sends a message with a video attached:
- Turn is dispatched to `xiaomi/mimo-v2.5`
- Response is returned normally in the same conversation thread
- Next text-only message resumes on `deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro`

---

## References

- MiMo-V2.5 official release: https://mimo.xiaomi.com/mimo-v2-5/
- MiMo-V2.5 on HuggingFace: https://huggingface.co/XiaomiMiMo/MiMo-V2.5
- MiMo-V2.5 on OpenRouter: https://openrouter.ai/xiaomi/mimo-v2.5
- DeepSeek V4 API docs: https://api-docs.deepseek.com/news/news260424
- Existing Xiaomi provider docs: https://docs.openclaw.ai/providers/xiaomi
- Related issue: #54367 (Native Xiaomi MiMo Ecosystem Integration)

---

## Checklist

- [ ] Add `mimo-v2.5` to built-in Xiaomi catalog (`input: ["text","image","video","audio"]`)
- [ ] Add `mimo-v2.5` to onboarding model selection UI
- [ ] Implement `multimodalFallback` key in agent model config
- [ ] Pre-dispatch input-type inspection (not error-triggered failover)
- [ ] Inject fallback response into history under primary model alias
- [ ] Update `https://docs.openclaw.ai/providers/xiaomi` with V2.5 catalog row
- [ ] Add migration note: V2.5 supersedes V2-Omni for new installs

- move isolated cron delete-after-run cleanup into a shared helper

- Title: fix(sessions): reduce session-store memory retention
- State: OPEN
- Author: efpiva
- Labels: docs, agents, size: L, plugin: file-transfer, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient, P1, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: 👀 ready for maintainer look

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

This PR addresses the dominant session-store memory-retention issue observed in a high-load CodeClaw/OpenClaw triage deployment. It keeps `loadSessionStore()` compatibility intact, adds safer immutable read APIs, bounds serialized cache state, and migrates several read-only paths away from mutable full-store clones.

Changes:

- Intern repeated large `SessionEntry.skillsSnapshot.prompt` strings after session-store parse/clone.
- Add immutable session-store read APIs:
  - `readSessionStoreSnapshot(storePath)`
  - `readSessionEntry(storePath, sessionKey)`
  - `readSessionEntries(storePath)`
- Export the snapshot read APIs through plugin SDK/runtime session helpers.
- Bound `SESSION_STORE_SERIALIZED_CACHE` by store-path count and total bytes.
- Migrate safe read-only paths to snapshot/entry reads:
  - cron isolated delivery target lookup
  - delivery-info lookup
  - `readSessionUpdatedAt()`
  - auto-reply verbose progress refresh
  - cron timer delivery context lookup
  - subagent announce output token/stat lookup
- Isolate small agent-attempt lifecycle callbacks to reduce unnecessary active-run closure capture.

## Problem / RCA

I have been dealing with an unstable OpenClaw instance under a high load of sessions, tasks, and `sessions_spawn` activity. After several memory dump analyses, I traced the dominant issue to session-store clone amplification: repeated full-store reads were duplicating large `skillsSnapshot.prompt` strings across hundreds of retained session-store clones.

The important workload characteristic is not raw request volume; it is session-store shape plus repeated access. CodeClaw had accumulated hundreds of session entries, especially `agent:main` subagent/topic sessions, each carrying a large `skillsSnapshot.prompt`. Before this fix, every compatible `loadSessionStore()` clone parsed/cloned the full store and created fresh copies of those large prompt strings. Active runs and gateway/session paths then retained enough clones for V8 old-space to fill.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: reduce retained session-store clone/prompt memory in a real high-session OpenClaw deployment while preserving `loadSessionStore()` compatibility and adding immutable read APIs for safer read paths.
- Real environment tested: live `codeclaw-triage` container running OpenClaw/CodeClaw with triage cron enabled, real persistent session stores, and normal cron/subagent review activity.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: captured and analyzed pre-fix OOM/near-OOM heap snapshots, deployed the session-store fix stack into `codeclaw-triage`, let triage cron run for about 20 minutes with the real session registry loaded, then captured a fresh SIGUSR2 heap snapshot and analyzed exact prompt strings/session-store map retainers.
- Evidence after fix: copied live heap-analysis output from the CodeClaw triage container is below; artifacts are saved under `/tmp/openclaw-session-store-evidence/sigusr2-capture-20260520T171954Z`.

  ```text
  Pre-fix captured heaps reached 4.1-4.5 GB.
  First analyzed OOM heap:
    heap nodes: ~50.6M
    exact skills prompt string nodes: ~389,465
    exact skills prompt bytes: ~1.5 GiB
    full-store clone-like maps: ~925

  Post-fix live codeclaw-triage SIGUSR2 heap after ~20 min cron/subagent load:
    heap snapshot size: 387,801,524 bytes (~370 MiB)
    heap nodes: 3.63M
    exact skills prompt string nodes: 8
    exact skills prompt bytes: 55,312 bytes
    full-store clone-like maps: 25 fingerprint / 14 strong candidates
    container restart=0
    OOMKilled=false
    serialized session-cache strings: ~11.35 MiB under 64 MiB cap
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the container stayed healthy during validation (`restart=0`, `OOMKilled=false`), and the catastrophic `skillsSnapshot.prompt` clone multiplier was gone; serialized session-cache strings were ~11.35 MiB under the new 64 MiB default cap.
- What was not tested: this does not claim all OpenClaw memory pressure is gone; remaining pressure includes active-run state, task registry retention, serialized session-state strings, and normal Node/runtime caches. Further locking down/deprecating mutable full-store reads is left for follow-up API discussion.

## Compatibility / open question

This PR intentionally preserves `loadSessionStore()` as a mutable-clone compatibility API. The new snapshot APIs provide a safer read path, and this PR migrates several known hot read-only consumers, but existing callers and plugins can still call `loadSessionStore()`.

Open question for reviewers: should we keep this non-breaking compatibility path and migrate call sites gradually, or start a deprecation/guardrail plan for mutable full-store reads? The stricter long-term model would reserve mutable access for explicit writer/maintenance paths and require snapshot/entry APIs for reads.

## Validation

```

github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_pr
- Number: 87802
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/87802
- Title: feat(opencode): add resolveDynamicModel and augmentModelCatalog hooks to Zen plugin
- State: CLOSED
- Author: olisikh
- Labels: size: M, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, extensions: opencode, P1, rating: 🦪 silver shellfish, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 auth-provider, status: 📣 needs proof

Body:
```markdown
# feat(opencode): add resolveDynamicModel and augmentModelCatalog hooks to Zen plugin

## Problem

The `opencode` (Zen) provider plugin, introduced in the OpenCode provider-key split, is missing `resolveDynamicModel` and `augmentModelCatalog` runtime hooks that **every other bundled provider implements**.

Without these hooks, the agent runtime **cannot resolve `opencode/<model-id>` references** at runtime. When a model like `opencode/deepseek-v4-flash-free` is configured in `agents.defaults.models`, the resolution chain:

1. ❌ Checks `modelRegistry.find("opencode", "deepseek-v4-flash-free")` — not found
2. ❌ Calls `plugin.resolveDynamicModel("opencode", "deepseek-v4-flash-free")` — **undefined** (hook not implemented)
3. ❌ Falls through to `resolveConfiguredFallbackModel` — requires explicit `models.providers["opencode"]` config entry
4. ❌ **"Unknown model" error**

Users hit this as `⚠️ Unknown model: opencode/deepseek-v4-flash-free` when trying to use the Zen proxy through fallback chains, requiring fragile config workarounds (manual `models.providers` entries or models.json manipulation) that are not needed for any other provider.

**Severity:** Medium — annoyance that requires manual config work for every Zen model the user wants to reference in the fallback chain.

## Solution

Add runtime hooks to the `opencode` Zen provider plugin, mirroring the pattern established by `opencode-go` at `extensions/opencode-go/provider-catalog.ts`:

### New file: `extensions/opencode/provider-catalog.ts`

Static model catalog with known Zen-proxied models:

| Model | API | Reasoning | Context |
|-------|-----|-----------|---------|
| deepseek-v4-flash-free | anthropic-messages | true | 65K |
| claude-opus-4-6 | anthropic-messages | true | 1M |
| claude-opus-4-7 | anthropic-messages | true | 1M |
| claude-sonnet-4-6 | anthropic-messages | true | 1M |
| claude-haiku-4-5 | anthropic-messages | true | 200K |

Catalog functions:
- `listOpencodeZenModelCatalogEntries()` — discoverable catalog entries
- `resolveOpencodeZenModel(modelId)` — runtime model resolution
- `normalizeOpencodeZenBaseUrl()` — base URL normalization (routes `anthropic-messages` → `/zen`, others → `/zen/v1`)
- `normalizeOpencodeZenResolvedModel()` — resolved model base URL normalization

### Modified: `extensions/opencode/index.ts` (+5 hooks)

- `resolveDynamicModel` → delegates to `resolveOpencodeZenModel()`
- `augmentModelCatalog` → delegates to `listOpencodeZenModelCatalogEntries()`
- `normalizeConfig` → normalizes provider config base URLs
- `normalizeResolvedModel` → normalizes resolved model base URLs
- `normalizeTransport` → normalizes transport-level base URLs

### New/modified manifest wiring: `extensions/opencode/openclaw.plugin.json`

Added `modelCatalog.runtimeAugment: true` to the plugin manifest. Without this, the real catalog loader (`src/plugins/providers.ts:100`) filters bundled augment hooks behind `modelCatalog.runtimeAugment`, so the `augmentModelCatalog` hook was wired in `index.ts` but never actually activated in production. This is the same pattern used by `extensions/anthropic/openclaw.plugin.json`.

### Resolution chain after fix

1. ✅ `modelRegistry.find("opencode", "deepseek-v4-flash-free")` — found (catalog provides it)
2. ✅ `plugin.resolveDynamicModel("opencode", "deepseek-v4-flash-free")` — resolves at runtime
3. (Escape hatch) No config workaround needed

## Verification

### Build & Tests

- `pnpm build` — clean
- `pnpm test -- extensions/opencode` — **20/20 tests passed** (6 test files)

```
 ✓  extension-misc  extensions/opencode/catalog-integration.test.ts (2 tests) 163ms
 ✓  extension-misc  extensions/opencode/index.test.ts (9 tests) 59ms
 ✓  extension-misc  extensions/opencode/plugin-registration.contract.test.ts (2 tests) 2ms
 ✓  extension-misc  extensions/opencode/onboard.test.ts (2 tests) 1ms
 ✓  extension-misc  extensions/opencode/media-understanding-provider.test.ts (3 tests) 1ms
 ✓  extension-misc  extensions/opencode/provider-policy-api.test.ts (2 tests) 0ms

 Test Files  6 passed (6)
      Tests  20 passed (20)
```

### New integration tests — real catalog pipeline (`catalog-integration.test.ts`)

Proves the manifest `runtimeAugment` is discovered by the real plugin manifest loader, and `resolveCatalogHookProviderPluginIds` returns `opencode` — confirming the catalog hooks are actually activated at runtime, not just registered in the provider entry:

```ts
it("manifest declares runtimeAugment and is picked up by real loader", () => {
  const registry = loadPluginManifestRegistry({});
  const opencodePlugin = registry.plugins.find(
    (p) => p.origin === "bundled" && p.id === "opencode",
  );
  expect(opencodePlugin?.modelCatalog?.runtimeAugment).toBe(true);
});

it("resolveCatalogHookProviderPluginIds includes opencode", () => {
  const result = resolveCatalogHookProviderPluginIds({ config: {}, env: {} });
  expect(result).toContain("opencode");
});
```

### Unit test coverage

- `resolveDynamicModel` resolves `deepseek-v4-flash-free` with correct api/reasoning/context metadata matching the live test
- `resolveDynamicModel` resolves `claude-opus-4-6` with `anthropic-messages` api
- `resolveDynamicModel` returns `undefined` for unknown model IDs (graceful fallback)
- `augmentModelCatalog` returns entries including both DeepSeek and Claude models

### Real pipeline proof

The `resolveCatalogHookProviderPluginIds` integration test above confirms that with `modelCatalog.runtimeAugment: true` in the manifest, the real `src/plugins/providers.ts` catalog-hook resolver includes `opencode` in its output. Without this manifest entry, the resolver would not return the plugin ID and `augmentModelCatalog` would never be called in production.

Rebased on `upstream/main` — clean, no merge conflicts. Fork synced.

## Why this matters

The `opencode` Zen provider is a multi-model proxy (Claude, GPT, Gemini, DeepSeek). Without `resolveDynamicModel`, every model requires a manual `models.providers` config entry — creating a poor developer experience and ongoing maintenance burden. With the hooks in place, models are automatically discoverable and resolvable, exactly like every other bundled provider.

## Change History

- **v4**: Added `openclaw.plugin.json` with `modelCatalog.runtimeAugment: true` so the real catalog loader activates the `augmentModelCatalog` hook. Added `catalog-integration.test.ts` proving manifest is picked up by real manifest registry and `resolveCatalogHookProviderPluginIds` includes opencode.
- **v3**: Claude models use `anthropic-messages` transport (not `openai-responses`). Claude context window: 1M, max tokens: 128K. Added `claude-haiku-4-5`. Removed stale `gemini-2.5-pro` (upstream has Gemini 3 family). `normalizeOpencodeZenBaseUrl` routes per API type (`anthropic-messages` → `/zen`, others → `/zen/v1`).
- **v2**: Fixed DeepSeek model metadata to match existing live test: `api: "openai-completions"`, `reasoning: true`, `contextWindow: 65_536`, `maxTokens: 8_192`. Added 4 unit tests for `resolveDynamicModel` and `augmentModelCatalog`.

Labels: size: M, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, extensions: opencode, P1, rating: 🦪 silver shellfish, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 auth-provider, status: 📣 needs proof
```

This PR fixes two proxy-related reliability issues for Discord gateway connections: it bootstraps a global `EnvHttpProxyAgent` so Node's `fetch()` honours `https_proxy`/`HTTP_PROXY` env vars, and disables `keepAlive` on all `ProxyAgent` / `EnvHttpProxyAgent` instances to prevent `ECONNRESET` from local proxies (Clash, Surge, etc.) that close HTTP CONNECT tunnels after a single request. - `run.ts`: adds `ensureGlobalUndiciEnvProxyDispatcher()` before `ensureGlobalUndiciStreamTimeouts()` so that the timeout options wrap the already-active `EnvHttpProxyAgent` (ordering comment is clear and correct). - `undici-global-dispatcher.ts`: `keepAlive: false` is added to both the initial proxy bootstrap and the timeout-wrapped `EnvHttpProxyAgent`. Per-request `dispatcher` overrides the global `EnvHttpProxyAgent`, so there is no double-proxy routing. - `rest-fetch.ts`: `ProxyAgent` constructor updated to pass `{ uri, connect: { keepAlive: false } }`. - Test files: mocks updated to accept the new constructor signature; `resolveEnvHttpProxyUrl` is mocked to `undefined` in existing gateway tests to isolate them from the env fallback.

- Safe to merge; one minor defensive-coding concern with spread order that poses no current risk. - The changes are focused, well-tested (10 + 8 unit tests), and the ordering of bootstrap calls is correct. The only concern is the `{ keepAlive: false, ...connect }` spread order in `ensureGlobalUndiciStreamTimeouts` — because `connect` currently never includes `keepAlive`, there is no active bug, but the ordering is fragile if `resolveConnectOptions` is ever extended. - `src/infra/net/undici-global-dispatcher.ts` line 126 — spread order of `proxyConnect`.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/infra/net/undici-global-dispatcher.ts Line: 126 Comment: **Spread order may allow `connect` to override `keepAlive: false`** The intent of `proxyConnect` is to always enforce `keepAlive: false` to prevent ECONNRESET from local proxies. However, using `{ keepAlive: false, ...connect }` means any `keepAlive` property that `connect` carries (e.g., if `resolveConnectOptions` is ever extended) would override the `false` value. target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #84660: [Bug] Voice STT: empty moonshine transcripts passed as raw JSON to LLM, clogging serialized processing queue
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 84660
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/84660
- Title: [Bug] Voice STT: empty moonshine transcripts passed as raw JSON to LLM, clogging serialized processing queue
- State: CLOSED
- Author: Joel-Claw
- Labels: P1, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:queueable-fix, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When using moonshine-tiny-en for Discord voice STT, empty/noisy transcripts are passed as raw JSON strings to the LLM instead of being filtered out. This wastes ~4 seconds and ~24k input tokens per empty segment and clogs the serialized processing queue, making the bot appear unresponsive in voice.

## Reproduction

1. Configure OpenClaw with `voice.mode = "stt-tts"` and moonshine-tiny-en as the STT model
2. Join a voice channel with background noise or short utterances
3. Observe that short/noisy audio segments produce empty transcripts: `{"lang": "", "emotion": "", "event": "", "text": "", "timestamps": [], "durations": [], "tokens":[], "ys_log_probs": [], "words": []}`
4. These empty JSON strings are sent to the LLM as "transcripts" instead of being filtered
5. The LLM returns NO_REPLY (correct behavior), but each call wastes ~4s and ~24k tokens
6. The serialized processing queue (`entry.processingQueue`) blocks until each call completes
7. With ~35% of segments being empty JSON, the pipeline appears to "stop" responding

## Root Cause

In `manager.runtime`, `transcribeVoiceAudio()` calls `normalizeOptionalString()` on the STT result, which returns `undefined` for empty strings. However, the sherpa-onnx CLI output includes the entire JSON object on the last line, and the `mediaUnderstanding.transcribeAudioFile()` result appears to include the full JSON string as `text` even when the `"text"` field within it is empty.

The check at line ~1441 (`if (!transcript)`) catches `undefined` but NOT the full JSON string with an empty `"text"` field. So `{"text": "", ...}` passes through as a non-empty string transcript.

## Evidence

Session logs show:
```
Voice transcript from speaker "[CK] Alex the 'guin":
{"lang": "", "emotion": "", "event": "", "text": "", "timestamps": [], "durations": [], "tokens":[], "ys_log_probs": [], "words": []}
```

100% of NO_REPLY responses (8 out of 8 in a recent session) were triggered by these empty JSON transcripts. The bot responded correctly to all real transcripts but was blocked during empty JSON processing.

52 segment files accumulated in 10 minutes. Only 10 TTS outputs were generated. The pipeline was processing empty JSON ~35% of the time.

## Expected Behavior

1. When the STT model returns `"text": ""` (or equivalent empty transcript), the segment should be skipped entirely — no LLM call needed
2. The serialized processing queue should have a max depth or stale-segment discard mechanism to prevent pipeline stalls

## Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.5.18
- sherpa-onnx moonshine-tiny-en (int8)
- Discord voice mode: stt-tts
- Platform: Linode 4 vCPU, 8GB RAM

## Workaround

Reducing `captureSilenceGraceMs` (from 1500 to 1000) and `timeoutSeconds` (from 300 to 120) helps marginally, plus periodic cleanup of stale `/tmp/openclaw/discord-voice-*/segment.wav` files. But the core issue is that empty transcripts should be filtered before reaching the LLM.
```

- State: OPEN
- Author: qingchejun
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:needs-live-repro, impact:crash-loop, issue-rating: 🐚 platinum hermit

Body:
```markdown
## Environment
- **OpenClaw:** 2026.3.23-1 (upgraded from 2026.3.13 during debug)
- **macOS:** Darwin 25.3.0 (arm64, Mac mini M4 Pro)
- **Node:** v25.5.0
- **Chrome:** 146.0.7680.155
- **chrome-devtools-mcp:** 0.20.3 (npm cache)
- **Browser profile:** `existing-session` driver, `attachOnly: true`

## Summary
Browser proxy (port gateway+2, e.g. 18791) accepts TCP connections but never responds — all requests timeout after 20s. Root cause: `npx -y chrome-devtools-mcp@latest` spawned by Gateway's `StdioClientTransport` hangs indefinitely — process starts but never produces child processes or MCP initialize response.

## Reproduction
1. Configure browser profile with `driver: "existing-session"`, `attachOnly: true`
2. Enable Chrome remote debugging (`chrome://inspect`)
3. Start Gateway
4. Call any browser tool endpoint (e.g. `GET /` on browser control port)
5. Request hangs until 20s timeout

## Root Cause Analysis

### Call chain that hangs
```
browser tool request
  → Express route handler (GET /, /tabs, /snapshot, etc.)
    → isReachable() [routes-B2QX_8fI.js:4011]
      → listChromeMcpTabs()
        → getSession() → createRealSession()
          → StdioClientTransport.spawn("npx", ["-y", "chrome-devtools-mcp@latest", ...])
            → npm exec process starts but NEVER produces child processes
              → MCP initialize response never arrives
                → await hangs forever → Express handler blocked → all requests timeout
```

### Spawn works outside Gateway, fails inside

| Context | Result |
|---------|--------|
| Manual shell: `npx -y chrome-devtools-mcp@latest --autoConnect` | ✅ Works, MCP init <2s |
| Node.js `child_process.spawn` (pipe stdio, same PATH/env/proxy vars) | ✅ Works |
| Node.js spawn with `cwd: "/"` | ✅ Works |
| Gateway internal spawn via `StdioClientTransport` | ❌ Hangs indefinitely |

### Orphan process accumulation
Each failed spawn leaves orphan processes (`PPID=1`):
- `openclaw-node` processes detach from Gateway
- `npm exec chrome-devtools-mcp` processes spawn under orphaned `openclaw-node`
- These orphans accumulate and are never cleaned up

### Proxy environment (NOT the cause)
Gateway runs with `http_proxy=http://127.0.0.1:8234` (needed for Telegram). Manual spawn with identical proxy vars works fine.

### Chrome CDP confirmed working
- Port 18800 listening (verified via `lsof` + WebSocket handshake)
- Manual `npx chrome-devtools-mcp --autoConnect` connects successfully
- Chrome consent dialog previously approved

## Workaround
`OPENCLAW_SKIP_BROWSER_CONTROL_SERVER=1` in Gateway start script disables browser control server entirely.

## Hypothesis
Something in Gateway's process management (signal handlers, child process tracking, or event loop state at spawn time) interferes with `npm exec`'s ability to resolve and execute the cached package. The spawned process appears stuck in package resolution — no child node process for the actual MCP server is ever created.
```

Comments/context:
```markdown
- DrDexter6000 at 2026-04-06T10:29:44Z:
## Cross-reference: Potential Root Cause - Node.js v24/v25 Windows Process Termination Bug

This issue describes browser control server hangs caused by npx chrome-devtools-mcp spawn failures. While this specific report is on macOS + Node v25, the StdioClientTransport spawn pattern is also affected by a **Node.js Windows runtime bug** that may explain similar failures on Windows.

The orphan process accumulation described in this issue - where processes detach from Gateway but never clean up - is consistent with what TerminateProcess() causes: the parent loses track of children that were killed externally, and cleanup logic never runs.

We independently verified this bug while debugging a Chrome Native Messaging host chain on Windows:

- Bare setInterval(() => {}, 1000) in detached spawn **dies in ~4-6 seconds** on v24.13.1
- **All exit handlers fail to fire** - TerminateProcess() bypasses all JS cleanup
- **Python equivalent survives indefinitely** - this is Node.js-specific
- **Node v22.22.2 LTS works** - 137+ seconds uptime verified

### Suggested investigation

If Windows users report browser control server issues, check their Node.js version. The workaround is to use Node v22 LTS on Windows.

- DrDexter6000 at 2026-04-06T12:26:01Z:
## Important Update: Windows Security Behavioral Detection Confirmed

After further investigation, we now have **direct evidence** that Windows Security (Defender) behavioral detection is killing detached child processes on Windows. This may be a contributing factor alongside any Node.js v24 runtime behavior.

### Evidence

On 2026-04-06 at 08:23 local time, while running minimal detached spawn reproduction tests with Node.js v24.13.1 on Windows 11, Windows Security flagged and terminated the test script:

- **Detection**: `Trojan:Win32/SuspExec.SE` (Suspicious Execution)
- **Status**: Removed (process killed)
- **Action**: The process was terminated immediately with no notification to the Node.js runtime

The Windows Security Protection History shows the exact command line that was flagged. The test script was a minimal reproduction: spawn a detached `node -e 'setInterval(()=>{},1000)'` child, then poll it with `process.kill(pid, 0)`.

### Why This Pattern Triggers Detection

Windows Security behavioral analysis flags this pattern because it resembles C2 beacon/persistence behavior:

1. PowerShell writes a script to disk
2. Script spawns a detached, never-exiting child process
3. Parent polls child liveness at regular intervals
4. Child has no visible I/O or user-facing activity

This is indistinguishable from malware persistence techniques at the behavioral level.

### Key Observation: Other Tools Work Fine

On the same machine, other browser automation tools work without issues:
- **Claude Code** (desktop): browser automation works perfectly
- **Manus AI** (web): browser automation works perfectly

This suggests the issue is not purely a Node.js v24 runtime bug. If it were, all Node.js-based tools would be equally affected.

### Revised Hypothesis

The root cause is likely a **combination of factors**:

1. **Windows Security behavioral detection** (confirmed): kills processes that match the SuspExec pattern, regardless of digital signature validity. The detection is behavior-based, not signature-based.
2. **Node.js v24 compatibility**: Windows Security may not have whitelisted/recognized the behavioral profile of Node.js v24 yet (newer runtime = fewer established behavioral patterns = higher false positive rate).
3. **Spawn pattern similarity to malware**: `detached: true + stdio: 'ignore' + child.unref()` + polling is exactly how many persistence implants work.

### Why Node v22 LTS Works

Node v22 LTS has been around longer. Windows Security has likely built behavioral whitelisting for common Node.js v22 spawn patterns. Node v24 is newer and its behavioral profile may not be fully recognized yet.

### Updated Workaround

In addition to using Node v22 LTS, also consider:

1. Add your project directory to **Windows Security exclusions** (Settings > Privacy & Security > Windows Security > Virus & Threat Protection > Exclusions)
2. Use Node v22 LTS for spawning long-lived detached processes: `nvm install 22 && nvm use 22`
3. If developing/testing detached spawn patterns on Windows, be aware that Windows Security real-time protection may silently kill your processes

This finding also explains the "invisible hand" effect we observed: processes dying after ~4-6 seconds with zero diagnostic output, zero JS exit handlers firing, and zero Event Log entries. Windows Security `TerminateProcess()` produces exactly this symptom profile.

- DrDexter6000 at 2026-04-06T13:18:14Z:
## ⚠️ Correction: Root Cause Was Windows Defender, Not Node v24 Bug

**TL;DR:** Our original diagnosis was wrong. The actual root cause was **Windows Defender behavior detection** (`Trojan:Win32/SuspExec.SE`), not Node.js v24 silently terminating detached processes.

### What Changed

After adding Windows Defender exclusions for `OpenCode.exe`, `opencode-cli.exe`, `node.exe`, and their install directories:

- **Node v24.13.1 detached spawn**: ✅ Stable 8+ seconds (previously died at 4-6s)
- **Chrome headless launch**: ✅ Normal (previously killed by Defender)
- **CDP browser automation (full chain)**: ✅ Tab create → navigate → JS eval → DOM modify → close — all pass

The same Node v24.13.1 binary, same code, same machine — the only change was Defender exclusions.

### Corrected Diagnosis

| Before (Wrong) | After (Correct) |
|----------------|-----------------|
| Node v24 silently terminates detached children via `TerminateProcess()` | Windows Defender behavior detection flags AI coding agent processes as `Trojan:Win32/SuspExec.SE` and kills them |
| Workaround: downgrade to Node v22 | Workaround: add Defender exclusions for AI coding tools |
| nodejs/node#62125 is the root cause | nodejs/node#62125 may still be a real issue under other conditions, but **our specific reproduction case was Defender** |

### Why the Misdiagnosis

1. `TerminateProcess()` was called — but by Windows Defender, not Node.js internals
2. No exit handlers fired — because Defender's `TerminateProcess()` is a forced kill, identical behavior to what we attributed to Node
3. Python survived — not because of language differences, but because Defender's heuristic didn't flag Python processes the same way
4. Digital signature was valid — Defender uses behavioral heuristics, not just signature checks

### Evidence Log

- Windows Security popup: `Trojan:Win32/SuspExec.SE` detected during debugging session
- Claude Code and Manus AI browser automation worked fine on same machine (different process signatures)
- After Defender exclusions: all tests pass, including bare `setInterval` detached spawn on v24.13.1

**Apologies for the initial misattribution.** The nodejs/node#62125 issue may still be valid for other reporters — we can only correct our own reproduction case.

---

- Type: github_pr

- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/79447
- Title: fix(model-auth): resolve per-entry apiKey profile ID references
- State: CLOSED
- Author: kinjitakabe
- Labels: agents, size: M, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient

Body:
```markdown
# fix(model-auth): resolve per-entry apiKey profile ID references

## Summary

A `models.providers.<id>.apiKey` value can be a **stored-profile-ID reference** (e.g. `"openrouter:key-b"`) rather than a literal bearer token. Without this PR, `resolveApiKeyForProvider`:

1. Builds `resolveAuthProfileOrder` for the per-entry provider (e.g. `openrouter-minimax`). The per-entry id has no auth alias to the canonical `openrouter`, so the resolved order is empty.
2. Falls through to the late `resolveUsableCustomProviderApiKey`, which returns the configured `apiKey` value as-is.
3. Sends `"openrouter:key-b"` itself to the upstream API as `Authorization: Bearer openrouter:key-b` → HTTP 401.

This PR adds a profile-ID dereference step in `resolveApiKeyForProvider`: when the per-entry `apiKey` matches a stored profile in the scoped auth-profile store, resolve that profile's actual credential before any literal-bearer code path runs.

The follow-up commit `cf0d87be7d` addresses the three findings raised by clawsweeper's review:
- [P3] Hoist the profile-ref check **before** the `shouldPreferExplicitConfigApiKeyAuth` early-return so a provider entry with both `auth: "api-key"` and `apiKey: "openrouter:key-b"` resolves the profile instead of sending the profile-ID string as a literal bearer.
- [P2] Make matched profile references **terminal**: a matched-but-unresolvable reference throws an explicit error instead of falling through to other credential paths that could silently use a different key or re-introduce the literal-bearer failure mode.
- [P1] Add a **credential-class compatibility check**: OAuth and AWS-SDK credentials are rejected when referenced from a per-entry provider that expects a bearer-style apiKey, since those credential classes are not bearer-shaped. Same-class cross-provider references are deliberately allowed because the explicit profile-ID reference is itself the user's safe-mapping declaration — that allowance is what makes the documented split-provider pattern work.

Fixes #67423.

## Root cause

[src/agents/model-auth.ts](src/agents/model-auth.ts) on pre-fix `main`:

```ts
const order = resolveAuthProfileOrder({ cfg, store, provider, preferredProfile });
// ... iterate profile order (empty for openrouter-minimax with no alias) ...

const customKey = resolveUsableCustomProviderApiKey({ cfg, provider });
if (customKey) {
  // openrouter-minimax.apiKey = "openrouter:key-b" returns here verbatim.
  return { apiKey: customKey.apiKey, source: customKey.source, mode: "api-key" };
}
```

`resolveUsableCustomProviderApiKey` at [src/agents/model-auth.ts:126](src/agents/model-auth.ts#L126) returns any non-marker `models.providers.<id>.apiKey` string as a `models.json` bearer token, including stored-profile-ID strings.

## What changed (scope boundary)

- **MOD** [src/agents/model-auth.ts](src/agents/model-auth.ts) — new helper `resolvePerEntryProviderApiKeyReference` returns a discriminated `PerEntryProfileReferenceResolution` (`no-match` / `matched-resolved` / `matched-incompatible` / `matched-failed`). The helper is invoked once at the top of `resolveApiKeyForProvider`, **before** any literal-bearer code path can run. A matched reference is terminal: it either returns the resolved auth or throws with an explicit error naming the matched profile.
- **MOD** [src/agents/auth-profiles.ts](src/agents/auth-profiles.ts) — re-export `isStoredCredentialCompatibleWithAuthProvider` from `./auth-profiles/order.js` so the model-auth path can reuse the same eligibility helper used by `resolveAuthProfileOrder`. Used inside the new helper for the OAuth/AWS-SDK class check.
- **MOD** [src/agents/model-auth.profiles.test.ts](src/agents/model-auth.profiles.test.ts) — six regression cases in `describe("resolveApiKeyForProvider — per-entry apiKey as profile ID reference")`:
  1. Resolves credential when per-entry apiKey matches a profile ID (the original #67423 case).
  2. Literal API key with no matching profile still works as-is.
  3. `auth.order.openrouter` does not bleed into a per-entry `openrouter-minimax` provider that has its own apiKey reference.
  4. Profile reference resolves even when the provider sets `auth: "api-key"` explicitly (regression for clawsweeper P3).
  5. OAuth credential routed to an api-key provider throws (clawsweeper P1).
  6. Matched profile that fails to resolve throws instead of falling through to literal-bearer (clawsweeper P2).
- **MOD** [CHANGELOG.md](CHANGELOG.md) — entry under `## Unreleased` › `### Fixes`.

## What did NOT change (scope boundary)

- **`resolveUsableCustomProviderApiKey`**: unchanged. The literal-bearer fallback still applies to literal apiKey values (regression test #2 confirms).
- **`resolveAuthProfileOrder` and the canonical auth-profile discovery path**: unchanged. Provider entries without a stored-profile-ID reference still flow through the existing eligibility/order resolution.
- **Strict provider-id aliasing**: the new helper deliberately uses a credential-class check rather than the strict id-equality check used by `resolveAuthProfileOrder`. The strict id check would reject `openrouter-minimax` resolving an `openrouter:key-b` profile reference — the exact case this PR was added to support — because `openrouter-minimax` is not aliased to `openrouter` in any manifest. The class check still blocks the truly dangerous cross-class shapes (OAuth tokens, AWS-SDK profiles as bearers). If stricter id-aliasing on top of this is wanted, that's an additive follow-up.
- **The explicit `auth: "api-key"` early-return path itself**: unchanged in shape, only repositioned relative to the new profile-ref check. Providers that set `auth: "api-key"` without a profile-ID reference behave exactly as before.
- **No new config knob, no plugin-SDK surface expansion.** `models.providers.<id>.apiKey` is the same documented field; we just stop sending a profile-ID string as a literal bearer.

## Regression test plan

`src/agents/model-auth.profiles.test.ts` — six cases in the per-entry-apiKey describe block, plus the rest of the existing 112-test file passing unmodified.

| # | Test | Asserts |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `resolves actual credential when per-entry apiKey matches a profile ID in the store` | Happy path — fix for #67423 works end-to-end |
| 2 | `does not treat a literal API key as a profile ID when no matching profile exists` | Literal-bearer regression guard — unchanged behavior when there's no profile match |
| 3 | `does not bleed auth.order canonical provider profiles into a per-entry provider` | Auth-order isolation — `auth.order.openrouter` does not select for `openrouter-minimax` |
| 4 | `resolves profile reference even when provider sets auth: api-key explicitly` | P3 regression — explicit `auth: "api-key"` no longer short-circuits the profile-ref path |
| 5 | `throws when matched profile is an OAuth credential routed to an api-key provider` | P1 — OAuth credential class is rejected with an explicit error |

github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_issue - Number: 60381 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/60381 - Title: browser tool: add force parameter for click and expose evaluate action - State: OPEN - Author: Gui-Ni - Labels: P2, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:queueable-fix, clawsweeper:source-repro, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster Body: ```markdown # Browser Tool Enhancement Proposal ## Problem Statement OpenClaw's `browser` tool, built on Playwright, frequently fails to interact with modern frontend frameworks (React/Vue/Svelte) due to strict hit-test and visibility checks during physical click simulation. ### Typical Failure Pattern ``` Error: Element is intercepted - A transparent overlay div is covering the button - The button uses `pointer-events: none` on a parent element - The element has CSS layout that offsets it visually but not in DOM coordinates - Animations/transitions haven't completed before the click is issued ``` This happens because `page.click()` simulates a real physical pointer interaction with full hit-testing, while modern frontend frameworks often build complex DOM structures that look interactive but fail Playwright's strict accessibility/visibility checks. --- ## Proposed Solutions ### Appendix: `javascript:` Goto Hack — Blocked An alternative escape hatch was discovered: using Playwright's `page.goto()` with a `javascript:` URL protocol to inject JS: ```json { "action": "goto", "url": "javascript:document.querySelector('[data-ref=\"e12\"]').click(); void(0);" } ``` **Finding**: ClawX Gateway explicitly rejects this with: ``` GatewayClientRequestError: Navigation blocked: unsupported protocol "javascript:" ``` The Gateway has already anticipated and blocked this vector. This confirms that a proper `evaluate` action must be implemented at the routing level, not workarounded through navigation. --- ## Proposed Solutions ### Solution A: Add `force` Parameter to `click` Action (Low Cost) Playwright natively supports `page.click(selector, { force: true })`, which skips all actionability checks (visibility, stability, animatable, receiving pointer events, etc.). **Implementation**: ```typescript // In browser tool's act handler case "click": const force = params.force === true; await page.click(selector, { force }); break; ``` **When to use**: When the element exists in the DOM but is visually "covered" by transparent layers, or when `pointer-events: none` parent elements block clicks. --- ### Solution B: Add `evaluate` Action (Escape Hatch) Expose Playwright's `page.evaluate()` directly to allow arbitrary JavaScript execution in the page context. **Implementation**: ```typescript case "evaluate": // script: string — JavaScript code to execute in browser context const result = await page.evaluate(params.script); return { success: true, result }; ``` **Capabilities unlocked**: - Direct DOM manipulation to trigger listeners bypassed by physical clicks - Read `window.localStorage` / `sessionStorage` - Extract obfuscated or framework-generated data - Directly call framework methods (e.g., React's `ref.current.click()`) - Bypass CORS restrictions in certain contexts **Example usage**: ```json { "action": "evaluate", "script": "document.querySelector('[data-testid=submit]').click()" } ``` --- ## API Changes ### Extended `act` Action Parameters ```typescript // click subtype { action: "click", ref: "e12", force?: boolean // NEW: skip hit-test and visibility checks } // NEW — evaluate subtype { action: "evaluate", script: string // JavaScript to execute in page context } ``` --- ## Behavior Recommendation | Scenario | Recommended Action | |----------|-------------------| | Simple visible button, no overlays | Default `click` | | Button covered by transparent div | `click` with `force: true` | | Framework-bound event (React `onClick`, Vue `@click`) | `evaluate` with `.click()` | | Need to read `localStorage` / app state | `evaluate` with return statement | | Complex multi-step trigger | `evaluate` with arbitrary JS | --- ## Implementation Pitfalls (暗坑) ### Pitfall 1: `evaluate` Return Value Serialization Playwright's `page.evaluate()` must serialize the return value back to Node.js/Python. **DOM elements cannot be serialized** — attempting to return a raw DOM node will throw or silently return `{}`. | ❌ Wrong | ✅ Correct | |----------|-----------| | `return document.querySelector('.btn')` | `return document.querySelector('.btn').innerText` | | `return window.reactRoot` | `return window.reactRoot.state` | | `return element.getAttribute('href')` | `return element.getAttribute('href')` | **Tool description must include this warning explicitly:** > `The return value of the script must be a primitive type or a JSON-serializable object. Do not return DOM elements or non-serializable objects.` **Implementation**: Add a serialization safety check in the evaluate handler: ```typescript case "evaluate": try { const result = await page.evaluate(params.script); // Verify result is serializable JSON.stringify(result); // will throw if not serializable return { success: true, result }; } catch (e) { return { success: false, error: e.message }; } ``` --- ### Pitfall 2: Model Laziness — `evaluate` Overuse High-intelligence models (GPT-4 class) tend to discover that `evaluate` is a powerful escape hatch and may start using it for **all** interactions instead of attempting normal `click` operations first. This degrades the fidelity of testing (synthetic events behave differently from real user interactions in edge cases). **Consequence**: Interactions that would work fine with `click` might silently fail in production because the model never actually tested the real user flow. **Mitigation — mandatory rule in system prompt:** ``` # 🛡️ Browser Interaction Fallback Rules If your `click` action fails due to "Element is intercepted", "pointer-events: none", or transparent overlays, DO NOT repeat the same click. Immediately adopt one of: 1. **Click the Parent/Child:** Target a structurally adjacent element. The inner ``, ``, or outer wrapper `
` of a button is often not intercepted. 2. **Keyboard Navigation:** If the element is blocked but focusable, click a nearby "safe" area, use `press` action to hit `Tab` until target is focused, then send `Enter` or `Space`. 3. **Scroll to Center:** Intercepted by sticky headers/footers? Attempt to scroll the element to the center of the viewport before clicking. 4. **force: true** (only after the above fail): Use `force: true` as a last-resort retry before falling back to `evaluate`. ``` **The Behavior Recommendation table should be embedded as a hard rule, not a suggestion.** --- ## Risk Assessment ### Security Considerations - `evaluate` allows arbitrary JS execution in the browser context — this is a powerful escape hatch that should be documented as a privileged operation - Suggest marking `evaluate` as a "advanced" or "fallback" action in tool descriptions - The browser profile is already isolated (`browser/openclaw/user-data`), so no cross-session contamination - Serialization validation prevents accidental data leakage through unserializable objects ### Backward Compatibility - Both changes are additive — no existing behavior is modified - `force` defaults to `false` (current behavior unchanged) - `evaluate` is purely new — no conflict with existing actions --- ## Implementation Checklist - [ ] Add `force?: boolean` parameter to click handler - [ ] Add new `evaluate` action case in act handler - [ ] Update tool description/schema to reflect new parameters - [ ] Add documentation in TOOLS.md - [ ] Add test cases for both features --- ## Priority Suggestion **Solution A (`force` parameter)** is trivial to implement and covers the majority of "Element is intercepted" failures. Recommend implementing first. **Solution B (`evaluate` action)** should be added alongside as a proper "escape hatch" for the remaining cases that `force` doesn't solve, and to unlock broader capabilities. ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - clawsweeper at 2026-04-28T17:34:05Z: Codex review: keeping this open for maintainer follow-up; there is still a little grit to resolve. **Workflow note:** Future ClawSweeper reviews update this same comment in place.

github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 84732
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/84732
- Title: Slack channel sends fail: `reconcileUnknownSend` required but no channel adapter implements it
- State: CLOSED
- Author: quanyeomans
- Labels: P2, impact:message-loss

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When an agent attempts to send a message into a Slack channel via the `message` tool, the call fails with:

```
Required durable message send is unsupported for slack: missing reconcileUnknownSend
```

DMs work because they use the natural assistant-emit path. Channel sends route through the `message` tool, which requires durable delivery, which requires `reconcileUnknownSend` — and no channel adapter implements it.

## Reproduction

- OpenClaw `2026.5.12` (also verified on `2026.5.19`)
- `@openclaw/slack` plugin `2026.5.12` (also verified on `2026.5.19`)
- Slack channel with bot invited + `chat:write` + `channels:history` scopes
- Agent uses the `message` tool with `target: "channel:<id>"`

Tool call payload that triggers it:

```json
{
  "action": "send",
  "channel": "slack",
  "target": "channel:C0XXXXX",
  "accountId": "consultant",
  "message": "[[reply_to_current]]some reply"
}
```

Stack location: `dist/message-22rGu__T.js` → `deriveRequiredMessageSendCapabilities` hardcodes:

```js
const requirements = { reconcileUnknownSend: true };
```

And asserts via `assertRequiredMessageSendDurability` → `resolveOutboundDurableFinalDeliverySupport`:

```js
if (required === true && capability === "reconcileUnknownSend" && typeof messageDurableFinal?.reconcileUnknownSend !== "function") return { ok: false, reason: "capability_mismatch", capability };
```

## What I observed

I greped every `channel-*.js` in `/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/`:

- `@openclaw/slack` declares `deliveryCapabilities.durableFinal: { text, media, payload, replyTo, thread, messageSendingHooks }` — no `reconcileUnknownSend`
- `@openclaw/mattermost` declares the same shape — no `reconcileUnknownSend`
- `@openclaw/msteams`, `@openclaw/tlon` — same
- Telegram adapter — same
- Only `dist/channel-message-CmG6T1ry.js` (the runtime, not an adapter) mentions `reconcileUnknownSend`

So the requirement is asserted but no shipping adapter satisfies it.

## Why it doesn't fire for other channels in practice

Other channels' agent sends apparently don't reach `queuePolicy === "required"`. The exact branching is non-obvious from the minified bundle, but our Telegram setup works end-to-end with the same channel-send pattern. Slack is hitting the `required` branch for some channel-targeted sends.

## Suggested fix

Either:

1. Drop the unconditional requirement and let adapters opt-in to `reconcileUnknownSend` when they actually implement it (the safest — matches the current actual ship state); or
2. Have `@openclaw/slack` provide a `message.durableFinal.reconcileUnknownSend` implementation. For Slack channel sends via `chat.postMessage`, the API is synchronous and returns `ts` immediately, so the unknown-send case is rare; a stub returning `{ status: "unresolved" }` would unblock; or
3. Add a `synchronousSend: true` capability to adapter declarations that, when set, skips the `reconcileUnknownSend` requirement.

## Local workaround we're running

```diff
- const requirements = { reconcileUnknownSend: true };
+ const requirements = {};
```

(plus the matching `reconcileUnknownSend: false` on the per-payload `deriveDurableFinalDeliveryRequirements` call).

Re-applied idempotently in our upgrade script. Detailed patch + rationale at:
`host/infra/patches/openclaw-slack-channel-reconcile.patch` in our deployment repo.

## Context

We're running a 6-agent Slack workspace with Socket Mode + a shared Slack Connect channel with an external collaborator. The agents process the external collaborator's messages cleanly (we can see the model output in the journal) but the response delivery dies at this capability check. Telegram-only worked; Slack was added on top recently.

Happy to PR option 1 against your tree if helpful.
```

```markdown
## Summary
- Add `LINE_OUTBOUND_MEDIA_MAX_BYTES` (image `originalContentUrl` 10 MB,
  video / audio `originalContentUrl` 200 MB, `previewImageUrl` 1 MB;
  verified against the LINE Messaging API reference on 2026-05-20) and
  `precheckLineOutboundMediaSize` in
  `extensions/line/src/outbound-media.ts`. The helper issues a HEAD via
  `fetchWithSsrFGuard` (5 s, `requireHttps`, `mode: "strict"`, reusing
  the existing `LINE_OUTBOUND_MEDIA_SSRF_POLICY` —
  `allowPrivateNetwork: false`).
- Hard-fail on `200/206` with `Content-Length > cap`. Soft-fail
  (`logVerbose` + pass through) on probe error, non-2xx status, absent
  / malformed / negative `Content-Length`. Logs strip the query string.
- Wire the precheck next to every existing `validateLineMediaUrl` site:
  `resolveLineOutboundMedia`, the video / audio / image branches in
  `sendMessageLine`, and `pushImageMessage`. `previewImageUrl` is
  always checked against the 1 MB preview cap (it is strictly smaller
  than the `originalContentUrl` cap, so LINE rejects oversized previews
  even when the original would pass).
- Dedupe: when `previewImageUrl === mediaUrl` (or the implicit fallback
  produces an equal URL), issue a single HEAD probe and evaluate it
  against the **stricter** preview cap — one network round-trip, both
  LINE-side validations covered.

## Behavior change
Outbound LINE media sends whose host reveals a body larger than the
LINE per-role cap now fail locally with a descriptive error. Today the
LINE platform fetches the URL asynchronously after our `pushMessage` /
`replyMessage` call returns, so an oversize file produces no synchronous
error on the caller side. Hosts that don't expose `Content-Length` keep
working unchanged — the precheck logs and passes through.

## Notes
- The adapter (`extensions/line/src/outbound.ts`, untouched by this
  PR) chains `resolveLineOutboundMedia` → `sendMessageLine`. Both
  layers now precheck, so the kind-aware adapter path issues two
  HEAD probes per media URL — the same shape as the existing
  `validateLineMediaUrl` duplication. Acceptable for the
  user-driven outbound media path; a future PR can collapse it once
  `outbound.ts` is in scope.
- Format / dimensions / duration validation is out of scope. Only
  size is gated.

## Non-Scope
- No changes to `src/media/**` or `src/plugin-sdk/**`. Independent
  of #76566 (MIME helpers — no file overlap).
- No new account-level config knob.
- Generic quick-reply inline media in `outbound.ts` is intentionally
  out of scope for this PR and should be tracked separately.

## Real behavior proof

### HEAD identity at current head

- Branch: `pr/line-media-size-precheck`
- Current HEAD: `ca3b45744e1c87f4b5ef70dda67cf5d8effeb619`
- Commits on top of base (3):
  - `df04af95e2 fix(line): precheck outbound media size` (precheck)
  - `37c6fae5ac fix(line): enforce LINE preview image size cap` (preview-cap)
  - `ca3b45744e fix(line): apply preview cap to implicit-preview image paths` (implicit-preview)

### Rebase / current-HEAD validity note

The branch was rebased onto `upstream/main` before the L1 proof window;
the rebase brought 22 upstream commits onto the base. None of those
upstream commits touched the four files in this PR
(`extensions/line/src/outbound-media.ts`, `outbound-media.test.ts`,
`send.ts`, `send.test.ts`).

The L1 live LINE proof (cases A–D below) was captured for the
pre-`ca3b45744e` HEAD. Commit `ca3b45744e` modifies
`outbound-media.ts` (+6 / -3) and `send.ts` (+6 / -4), but the
modifications affect only the **implicit-preview** branching in
`resolveLineOutboundMedia` (image kind, no explicit `previewImageUrl`)
and `createImageMessage` / `pushImageMessage` (no explicit
`previewImageUrl`). Cases A–D supply an **explicit** `previewImageUrl`
(equal to mediaUrl in A / D / D-push, distinct small / oversized in
B / C); the explicit-path code those cases exercise is unchanged by
`ca3b45744e`. Cases A–D therefore remain valid for current HEAD.

The new implicit-preview behavior added by `ca3b45744e` is proven
separately by L2 (terminal proof, current HEAD) — see "Live proof at
current HEAD" below.

### Exact cap values (verified against developers.line.biz/en/reference/messaging-api/ on 2026-05-20)

| Role | Cap (bytes) |
|---|---|
| image `originalContentUrl` | 10 485 760 |
| video `originalContentUrl` | 209 715 200 |
| audio `originalContentUrl` | 209 715 200 |
| `previewImageUrl` (image / video) | 1 048 576 |

### Live proof (LINE Messaging API) — L1, captured pre-`ca3b45744e`, valid for current HEAD per the note above

Driver imports `dist/extensions/line/runtime-api.js` from the rebuilt
worktree, so the live cases exercise the production precheck path
including the preview-cap wiring.

| Case | What | Result |
|---|---|---|
| A | `sendMessageLine` image, `previewImageUrl === mediaUrl`, file = 3 116 B | LINE app received the message; fixture origin log shows 1 HEAD (equal-URL dedupe) then LINE-side GET. |
| B | `sendMessageLine` image, `originalContentUrl` 14 545 753 B (>10 MiB) + valid small preview | Local `Error: LINE image media must be ≤10485760 bytes (got 14545753 bytes from <FIXTURE_HOST>/image-overcap.png)`; `client.pushMessage` never invoked. Origin log shows 1 HEAD on the over-cap original (no preview HEAD, no LINE-side GET). |
| C | `sendMessageLine` image, valid small `originalContentUrl` + `previewImageUrl` = 1 489 176 B (between 1 MiB and 10 MiB) | Local `Error: LINE preview media must be ≤1048576 bytes (got 1489176 bytes from <FIXTURE_HOST>/mid-preview-overcap.jpg)`; `client.pushMessage` never invoked. **This is the key proof for the preview-cap commit.** |
| D | `sendMessageLine` image, `previewImageUrl === mediaUrl` (explicit), shared file = 1 489 176 B | Local `Error: LINE preview media must be ≤1048576 bytes ...`; exactly **1 HEAD** for the shared URL (dedupe preserved, stricter cap applied). |
| D-push | `pushImageMessage` with `previewImageUrl === originalContentUrl` (explicit), shared file = 1 489 176 B | Same shape as D — 1 HEAD, preview-cap rejection. |

Fixture host was a short-lived Cloudflare quick-tunnel; tunnel torn
down after the proof window. No signed-URL query strings were used.
The named LINE webhook tunnel was not touched.

### Live proof at current HEAD — L2, terminal proof for `ca3b45744e` (implicit-preview cases)

Driver is vitest scoped via `-t` to the two regression tests added in
`ca3b45744e`; the production code path under test
(`resolveLineOutboundMedia` image branch / `pushImageMessage` /
`createImageMessage` implicit-preview wiring) is the source at current
HEAD. The `fetchWithSsrFGuard` HEAD-probe call is mocked so that
production precheck logic exercises the stricter 1 MiB preview cap
against the originally supplied URL with a controlled Content-Length —
no real LINE / external network call. Artifact directory (local,
gitignored; redactions applied per "Redactions" below):

```
.local/line-proof/L2/20260520T134418Z/
```

| Case | What | Result |
|---|---|---|
| L2-E | `resolveLineOutboundMedia` image kind, **no explicit `previewImageUrl`**, mediaUrl Content-Length = 5 MiB (5 242 880 B, under 10 MiB image cap, over 1 MiB preview cap) | Production throws `LINE preview media must be ≤1048576 bytes (got 5242880 bytes ...)`; `fetchWithSsrFGuard` called exactly **1×** (no double-probe on the shared implicit URL). |
| L2-F | `pushImageMessage` with **no explicit `previewImageUrl`**, `originalContentUrl` Content-Length = 3 MiB (3 145 728 B, under 10 MiB image cap, over 1 MiB preview cap) | Production throws `LINE preview media must be ≤1048576 bytes (got 3145728 bytes ...)`; `fetchWithSsrFGuard` called exactly **1×**, and `client.pushMessage` is asserted **not** to have been called. |

Both L2 vitest runs report `1 passed | 18 (or 38) skipped`, with the ✓
marker recorded against the regression test name verbatim — see
`.local/line-proof/L2/20260520T134418Z/40-verbose.txt`.

### Automated proof (at current HEAD `ca3b45744e`)

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/line/src/outbound-media.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/line/src/send.test.ts
OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=1 node scripts/check-changed.mjs
```

| Gate | Result |
|---|---|
| outbound-media.test.ts | 39 / 39 passed |
| send.test.ts | 19 / 19 passed |
| check:changed | exit 0 (typecheck, oxlint 0 warn / 0 err, runtime import cycles 0, all guards PASS) |

The preview-cap commit (`37c6fae5ac`) adds 4 asserts to
`outbound-media.test.ts` (explicit-preview rejection in
`resolveLineOutboundMedia`, explicit equal-URL shared-file rejection,
`kind=preview` unit-level rejection, `kind=preview` inclusive boundary)
and 2 asserts to `send.test.ts` (`sendMessageLine` image branch
preview-cap rejection + `pushImageMessage` explicit preview-cap
rejection). The implicit-preview commit (`ca3b45744e`) adds 1 further
assert to each file (L2-E and L2-F). T12 (the pre-existing oversize
image smoke) was widened to accept either the image or preview
rejection message since the implicit `previewIm

## Bug Report: Kimi Code Model Infinite Tool Call Loop

### Summary
The Kimi Code model (via `kimi-coding` provider) enters an infinite loop when processing tool results, repeatedly calling the same tools with identical parameters instead of generating a final answer.

### Environment
- **OpenClaw Version**: 2026.4.22
- **Provider**: `kimi` (Kimi Code)
- **API**: `anthropic-messages`
- **Model**: `kimi-code`

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Start a conversation with an agent using Kimi Code model
2. Send a request that requires tool calls (e.g., "Check Fast Agent configuration")
3. The model will:
   - Call tools correctly (e.g., `read`, `exec`)
   - Receive tool results
   - **Fail to generate final answer**
   - Repeat the same tool calls indefinitely

### Observed Behavior
Session logs show the pattern:
```
[ASSISTANT] stopReason="toolUse" tools=['read', 'exec']
[TOOLRESULT] models.json content
[TOOLRESULT] auth-profiles.json content
[ASSISTANT] stopReason="toolUse" tools=['read', 'exec']  ← Same calls!
[TOOLRESULT] models.json content (same)
[TOOLRESULT] auth-profiles.json content (same)
... repeats 50+ times
```

The assistant message content is **identical** on every iteration:
```
"Fast Agent is responsive. Let me continue checking its configuration:"
```

### Expected Behavior
After receiving tool results, the model should:
1. Process the results
2. Generate a final answer with `stopReason="stop"`
3. **Not** repeat tool calls

### Root Cause Analysis

The bug is in the Kimi stream wrapper at:
**File**: `extensions/kimi-coding/stream.ts` (compiled to `dist/stream-UsPs_HH1.js`)

**Function**: `rewriteKimiTaggedToolCallsInMessage`

**Problematic Code**:
```typescript
if (typedMessage.stopReason === "stop") {
    typedMessage.stopReason = "toolUse";
}
```

This logic **unconditionally** rewrites `stopReason` from `"stop"` to `"toolUse"` for ALL messages, including:
- ✅ Assistant generating tool calls (correct)
- ❌ **Assistant generating final answer after tool results** (incorrect!)

#### Why This Causes a Loop

1. User sends request → Kimi generates tool calls → `stopReason="stop"` (native)
2. OpenClaw wrapper rewrites to `"toolUse"` → tools execute → results returned
3. Kimi processes tool results → generates final answer → `stopReason="stop"` (native)
4. **BUG**: OpenClaw wrapper rewrites to `"toolUse"` again!
5. OpenClaw sees `stopReason="toolUse"` → expects more tool calls
6. But no new tool calls exist → system re-executes previous tools
7. **Loop begins**

#### The Fix

The rewrite should only happen when the message **actually contains tool calls**:

```typescript
if (typedMessage.stopReason === "stop" && typedMessage.role === "assistant") {
    const hasToolCalls = nextContent.some(block => block && block.type === "toolCall");
    if (hasToolCalls) {
        typedMessage.stopReason = "toolUse";
    }
}
```

### Impact
- **User Experience**: Agents appear unresponsive or stuck
- **API Costs**: Wasted tool executions (50+ repetitions observed)
- **Reliability**: Kimi Code model unusable for multi-turn tool use

### Workarounds
None known. The fix requires modifying the Kimi stream wrapper.

### Related Issues
- PR #60051: "parse kimi tagged tool calls" (related to Kimi tool call parsing)
- PR #70030: "stop strict-sanitizing Kimi's native tool_call IDs" (related to Kimi tool call handling)

### Verification
After applying the fix:
1. Send "Check Fast Agent configuration"
2. Agent calls tools (1-3 times)
3. Agent generates final report
4. **No repetition** ✅

---

**Labels**: `bug`, `provider:kimi`, `tool-use`, `infinite-loop`

```

Body:
```markdown
# Track provider-aware automatic TTS emotion mapping from #75043

## Summary

Track and review the provider-aware automatic TTS emotion mapping proposed in #75043.

The PR adds an opt-in `messages.tts.autoEmotion` config that infers a conservative abstract emotion from synthesized text, then maps that abstract emotion to provider-native TTS controls at the speech provider boundary.

Related PR: #75043
Related issue: #67539

## Problem

OpenClaw TTS providers expose expressiveness through different APIs:

- OpenAI TTS can use model-specific `instructions` on supported models.
- Microsoft and Azure Speech expose prosody controls such as rate, pitch, and volume.
- ElevenLabs exposes voice settings.
- Volcengine and Xiaomi expose provider-specific emotion/style surfaces.

Today users can configure provider-specific behavior manually, but there is no shared opt-in mechanism for lightweight, context-sensitive emotional variation when the user has not already selected an explicit provider emotion/style/prosody.

## Proposal in #75043

#75043 implements a conservative shared `autoEmotion` layer in speech-core:

1. `extensions/speech-core/src/tts.ts` infers an abstract emotion from the final synthesized text.
2. Speech-core checks for explicit overrides first, including provider config, persona provider bindings, trusted request overrides, and allowed model directives.
3. If no explicit emotion-equivalent setting is present, speech-core maps the abstract emotion into provider-native overrides.
4. Provider adapters remain responsible for translating those overrides into the actual provider request.

The new public config is:

```json
{
  "messages": {
    "tts": {
      "autoEmotion": {
        "enabled": true,
        "fallback": "neutral",
        "allowed": ["happy", "calm", "sad"]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

The option is disabled by default. Existing TTS behavior should remain unchanged unless users enable `messages.tts.autoEmotion.enabled`.

## Precedence and safety model

The PR is designed so explicit user/provider intent wins over inferred behavior:

- Persona provider bindings win over auto emotion.
- Provider config wins over auto emotion.
- Trusted request overrides win over auto emotion.
- Allowed model-provided TTS directives win over auto emotion.
- Auto emotion only fills the gap when no equivalent provider-specific emotion/style/prosody is already set.

This keeps the feature opt-in and avoids overriding intentionally configured voices or personas.

## Provider mapping shape

The PR maps inferred abstract emotions into provider-owned controls:

- OpenAI: `instructions`
- Microsoft: `rate`, `pitch`, `volume`
- Azure Speech: `rate`, `pitch`, `volume`
- ElevenLabs: `voiceSettings`
- Volcengine: `emotion`
- Xiaomi: `style`

The latest PR head also fixes the telephony path so OpenAI telephony preserves merged `instructions`, and Azure telephony forwards prosody overrides to `azureSpeechTTS`.

## Public surface touched

#75043 updates the full public configuration and documentation surface, including:

- `src/config/types.tts.ts`
- `src/config/zod-schema.core.ts`
- `src/config/schema.help.ts`
- `src/config/schema.labels.ts`
- `src/plugin-sdk/config-contracts.ts`
- `src/tts/tts-types.ts`
- `docs/tools/tts.md`
- provider adapters/tests under `extensions/`
- changelog entry for the user-facing config option

## Relationship to #67539

#67539 asks for provider-specific TTS prompt hints so agents know which expressive syntax is valid for the active provider. That direction is complementary but different:

- #67539 teaches the model/provider prompt what expressive syntax it may emit.
- #75043 applies deterministic runtime mapping after text is selected, without requiring the model to emit provider-specific syntax.

The main product-boundary question is whether #75043 should land as a shared speech-core `autoEmotion` policy, whether #67539 should land first as a narrower provider-owned prompt-hint seam, or whether both should exist because they solve different parts of TTS expressiveness.

## Current PR state

Latest checked PR head: 759367c7beeb74051512742cd07d3b7e70758014

Recent validation on #75043 included:

- `pnpm test extensions/openai/speech-provider.test.ts extensions/azure-speech/speech-provider.test.ts extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm lint`
- targeted `oxfmt --check`
- `git diff --check`

GitHub CI was also green at the latest check, with no failing checks.

## Open owner questions

1. Should `messages.tts.autoEmotion` be accepted as a shared speech-core feature, or should provider-specific expressiveness stay provider-owned only?
2. Is the current precedence model sufficient to protect personas and explicit provider configuration?
3. Should the PR be kept as one coherent feature, or split into a smaller provider-hint seam first and a later auto-emotion follow-up?
4. Do TTS/provider owners want updated real behavior proof against the latest PR head before review, beyond the focused provider/unit/type/lint validation already supplied?

## Expected decision

If owners agree with the shared opt-in policy, #75043 is the implementation PR for this issue. If owners prefer the narrower prompt-hint direction from #67539 first, this issue can track reshaping #75043 into a smaller follow-up after the provider hint seam lands.
```

Body:
```markdown
# Custom anthropic-messages providers missing Claude thinking profiles (adaptive/max)

## Custom `anthropic-messages` providers don't get Claude thinking profiles

### Problem

Custom providers configured with `api: "anthropic-messages"` (e.g. proxied through LiteLLM, Bifrost, or any Anthropic-compatible gateway) don't receive the Claude thinking profile from `resolveClaudeThinkingProfile`. This means:

- `/think adaptive` is rejected with "Use one of: off, minimal, low, medium, high"
- `/think max` is rejected on Sonnet 4.6 and Opus 4.6
- The `thinkingDefault: "adaptive"` agent config silently downgrades to `medium`

The root cause is that `resolveThinkingProfile()` in `thinking-*.js` only gets the Claude-specific profile when the **bundled anthropic plugin** registers `resolveThinkingProfile` as a provider hook. Custom providers with `api: "anthropic-messages"` get the correct transport format but never hit this hook — they fall through to `buildBaseThinkingProfile()` which only includes `off/minimal/low/medium/high`.

### Who this affects

Anyone routing Claude through a proxy or custom endpoint:
- Bifrost (`baseUrl: "http://proxy:4000/anthropic"`)
- LiteLLM
- AWS Bedrock via custom provider (not the bundled bedrock plugin)
- Any `anthropic-messages` API-compatible gateway

### Suggested fix

In `resolveThinkingProfile()`, after the plugin hook returns nothing, check if the model ID matches a known Claude model and call `resolveClaudeThinkingProfile()` as a fallback:

```js
// After pluginProfile check fails:
const _claudeProfile = resolveClaudeThinkingProfile(context.modelId);
if (_claudeProfile && _claudeProfile.levels && _claudeProfile.levels.length > 0) {
    return normalizeThinkingProfile(_claudeProfile);
}
```

`resolveClaudeThinkingProfile` already exists in `provider-model-shared` and correctly handles all Claude model families — it just isn't called for custom providers.

### Second issue: `max` effort missing for Sonnet 4.6 / Opus 4.6

`resolveClaudeThinkingProfile` returns `[...BASE_CLAUDE_THINKING_LEVELS, { id: "adaptive" }]` for the `CLAUDE_ADAPTIVE_THINKING_DEFAULT_MODEL_PREFIXES` family (Sonnet 4.6, Opus 4.6), but Anthropic's docs explicitly state these models support `max` effort:

> The `effort` parameter supports effort levels: `low`, `medium`, and `high`. For Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, and Sonnet 4.6, the parameter also supports `max` effort level. Use this parameter with or without enabling thinking.

Source: [Azure AI Foundry / Anthropic docs](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/anthropic/concepts/claude-models) and [Anthropic API docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/extended-thinking)

The fix is to add `{ id: "max" }` to the levels array for the adaptive-default model family:

```js
if (isClaudeAdaptiveThinkingDefaultModelId(modelId)) return {
    levels: [...BASE_CLAUDE_THINKING_LEVELS, { id: "adaptive" }, { id: "max" }],
    defaultLevel: "adaptive"
};
```

### Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.5.18
- Custom provider with `api: "anthropic-messages"`, `baseUrl` pointing at Bifrost proxy
- Models: claude-sonnet-4-6, claude-opus-4-6 via proxy

Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:auth-provider, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster
```

Body:
```markdown
# Feature Request: Per-agent default model and reasoning_effort in ACPX plugin config

## Problem

When OpenClaw spawns ACP sessions via `sessions_spawn` or `/acp spawn`, there is no way to configure default `model` or `reasoning_effort` per agent harness. Each new session starts with the agent's built-in default model, requiring manual post-spawn configuration via `/acp model` and `/acp set reasoning_effort` every time.

This is particularly painful for teams running multiple harnesses (e.g., Codex + Claude Code + Gemini CLI) where each needs a different model and reasoning configuration.

## Current State

### acpx CLI already supports `--model`

The acpx CLI has a `--model <id>` global option that applies the model via `session/set_config_option` after session creation. The `set` subcommand also supports `reasoning_effort` and other config options at runtime:

```bash
acpx --model gpt-5.4 codex prompt --session <name> --file -
acpx codex set reasoning_effort xhigh --session <name>
```

### OpenClaw ACPX plugin does not pass `--model`

The ACPX plugin's `buildPromptArgs()` constructs the following command:

```
acpx --format json --json-strict --cwd <dir> \
     --approve-reads --non-interactive-permissions <policy> \
     --ttl <ttl> <agent> prompt --session <name> --file -
```

No `--model` flag is included, and no `setConfigOption` call is made after session creation.

### Plugin config schema has no model/reasoning keys

`AcpxPluginConfigSchema` (strictObject) only supports:

- `command`, `expectedVersion`, `cwd`
- `permissionMode`, `nonInteractivePermissions`
- `pluginToolsMcpBridge`, `strictWindowsCmdWrapper`
- `timeoutSeconds`, `queueOwnerTtlSeconds`
- `mcpServers`

### Agent config overrides only support `command`

`readConfiguredAgentOverrides()` reads only the `command` field from agent entries, ignoring any other properties.

### No programmatic `/acp set` from agents

Agents cannot call `/acp model` or `/acp set` programmatically. These are user-facing slash commands only. `sessions_send` to an ACP session sends a new prompt turn, not a control command.

## Proposal

Add an `agentDefaults` key to the ACPX plugin config schema that allows per-agent default `model` and `configOptions`:

```json5
{
  "plugins": {
    "entries": {
      "acpx": {
        "config": {
          "agentDefaults": {
            "codex": {
              "model": "gpt-5.4",
              "configOptions": {
                "reasoning_effort": "xhigh"
              }
            },
            "claude": {
              "model": "anthropic/claude-opus-4-6",
              "configOptions": {
                "reasoning_effort": "high"
              }
            },
            "gemini": {
              "model": "gemini-3.1-pro"
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Implementation options

**Option A — Pass `--model` at prompt time (minimal change):**

In `buildPromptArgs()`, if `agentDefaults[agent].model` is set, append `--model <id>` to the prefix args. This leverages existing acpx CLI `--model` behavior with a one-line change.

**Option B — Auto `setConfigOption` after session creation (more flexible):**

After `ensureSession()` completes (or on first prompt), automatically call `setConfigOption()` for each key in `agentDefaults[agent].configOptions`. This handles arbitrary config options beyond just `model` (e.g., `reasoning_effort`, `mode`, future agent-specific options).

**Recommendation:** Combine both — Option A for `model` (since acpx already supports `--model`), Option B for `configOptions` (generic key-value pairs applied via `session/set_config_option`).

## Current Workaround

After each ACP session spawn, the operator must manually run:

```
/acp model gpt-5.4
/acp set reasoning_effort xhigh
```

This cannot be automated at the config level and must be repeated for every new session.

## Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.4.1
- ACPX plugin: bundled with OpenClaw
- Agents tested: codex (codex-acp 0.9.5), claude (claude-agent-acp 0.21.0), gemini CLI

```

## Real behavior proof
