target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #84110: [Bug]: Codex app-server rewrites prompt on tool-call continuation turns, busting OpenAI prompt cache mid-turn (cache ratio 93% → 47%)
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 84110
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/84110
- Title: [Bug]: Codex app-server rewrites prompt on tool-call continuation turns, busting OpenAI prompt cache mid-turn (cache ratio 93% → 47%)
- State: OPEN
- Author: danielsan1
- Labels: bug, P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:needs-live-repro, impact:auth-provider, issue-rating: 🐚 platinum hermit

Body:
```markdown
### Bug type

Regression (worked before, now fails)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

  Since OC v2026.5.12 + codex-cli 0.130.0 (first observed 2026-05-15), the Codex app-server rewrites the full prompt between a user turn
  and its tool-call continuation turn instead of appending only the tool_call_output, invalidating the OpenAI prompt cache on every exec
  tool call and raising effective per-token costs by ~3.5×.

### Steps to reproduce

  1. Run OpenClaw 2026.5.12 with model openai/gpt-5.4, Codex runtime (default, no agentRuntime.id override), auth via openai-codex OAuth
  profile.
  2. Send any message that triggers a custom_tool_call name=exec (e.g. a shell command via a cron job or assistant task).
  3. Observe cached=0 on the turn immediately following the exec tool call.
  4. Note that the input token count on the continuation turn is ~1.1k tokens smaller than on the preceding user turn — indicating a full
   prompt reorganization, not a simple append.
  5. Repeat across multiple tool calls in the same session: every tool-call continuation shows cached=0; cache never recovers within the
  session.
  6. Compare against a pre-update session (codex-cli < 0.130.0, same model/config): continuation turns show cached=1, cache ratio ~93%.

### Expected behavior

  Pre-update behavior (≤ 2026-05-14, codex-cli < 0.130.0): Codex app-server appends tool_call_output to the existing cached prompt
  prefix, preserving the OpenAI prompt cache across tool-call continuations. Session-wide cache ratio: ~93% (measured across 15 sessions,
   2026-05-10 to 2026-05-14).

### Actual behavior

  Post-update (≥ 2026-05-15, OC v2026.5.12 / codex-cli 0.130.0): On every turn following custom_tool_call name=exec (or
  function_call:wait), the Codex app-server rewrites the full prompt rather than appending. Result: cached=0 on all tool-call
  continuation turns. Input token count shrinks by ~1.1k tokens vs. the preceding turn — a reorganization signal, not an append.
  
  Session-wide cache ratio drops from ~93% to ~47–49%. Effective per-token cost is ~3.5× higher.
  
  Root cause confirmed NOT to be dev_instructions / inbound_meta variation: all turn_contexts in the affected sessions are
  byte-identical, dev_len=37177 constant across all 9 cached=0 bust events in the analyzed problem session.
  
  Evidence: 35 sessions analyzed (2026-05-10 to 2026-05-18). 100% of mid-turn cache busts occur immediately after custom_tool_call
  name=exec or function_call:wait. Zero mid-turn busts in pre-update sessions.

### OpenClaw version

2026.5.12 +

### Operating system

macOS 26.3.1 

### Install method

docker / orbstack

### Model

openai/gpt-5.x

### Provider / routing chain

openclaw -> openai-codex (ChatGPT-Plus OAuth) -> OpenAI Codex runtime

### Additional provider/model setup details

Auth profile: openai-codex (ChatGPT-Plus subscription OAuth — not API key).
  Runtime: Codex-Default (agentRuntime.id not set; Codex app-server is the effective runtime).
  thinkingDefault: medium, primary model openai/gpt-5.4 (alias GPT5).
  OPENAI_API_KEY env var present in environment but session auth resolves via OAuth profile.

  Note: PI-runtime workaround was attempted (agentRuntime.id: "pi") but was not effective in this environment due to env-OPENAI_API_KEY
  taking precedence over the OAuth profile in the PI code path (/status showed api-key instead of oauth despite registered openai-codex
  profile). This is a separate issue and NOT the subject of this report.

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell
Session-level cache bust pattern (post-update, representative session):
    Turn 1 (user message):       cached=1, input=~38k tokens
    Turn 2 (exec tool call):     cached=1
    Turn 3 (tool continuation):  cached=0, input=~36.9k tokens  ← bust, -1.1k tokens
    Turn 4 (user message):       cached=0
    Turn 5 (exec tool call):     cached=0
    Turn 6 (tool continuation):  cached=0  ← never recovers
   
  Pre-update comparison (same model, same config, codex-cli < 0.130.0):
    All continuation turns: cached=1, no token-count shrink observed.

  Aggregate (35 sessions):
    Pre-update  (15 sessions, 2026-05-10–14): cache ratio ~93%
    Post-update (20 sessions, 2026-05-15–18): cache ratio ~47–49%
    Mid-turn bust rate post-update: 100% of turns following custom_tool_call name=exec

  Analysis performed with: /tmp/bust_analyzer.py (Codex trajectory format), /tmp/post_aggregator.py (multi-session aggregation).
```

### Impact and severity

  Affected: All users running openai/gpt-5.4 (or any openai/gpt-* model) via Codex runtime with frequent exec/shell tool calls —
  particularly personal-assistant and cron-based workloads.
  Severity: High — blocks efficient use of ChatGPT-Plus subscription quota.
  Frequency: 100% reproducible post-update (every session, every exec tool call).
  Consequence: Effective per-token cost ~3.5× higher than pre-update; ChatGPT-Plus quota depleted ~3.5× faster for identical workloads.
  Users on API-key billing incur proportionally higher charges.


### Additional information

  Last known good: codex-cli < 0.130.0 / OC < 2026.5.12 (cache ratio ~93%)
  First known bad: codex-cli 0.130.0 / OC v2026.5.12 (cache ratio ~47–49%, first observed 2026-05-15)

  This matches the root cause of upstream issue #64217 (OPEN): "Source-dependent instructions/tools drift invalidates OpenAI prompt cache
   within the same session." That issue covers inter-turn drift at the user-instruction level; this report covers the same underlying
  mechanism (prompt rewriting instead of appending) triggered specifically by tool-call continuations in the Codex app-server.
  
  Related upstream issues for context: #66251 (umbrella), #82710 (app-server probe requests), #80131 (per-request tool bundling), #63030
  (same pattern on Anthropic-side).
  
  No effective client-side workaround found. PI-runtime path not available in this environment (see provider setup details above).
```

Comments/context:
```markdown

Greptile Summary

This PR lands the Exec V2 contract follow-through: new test coverage for Linux allowlist path/glob contracts, `safeBins` profile/trusted-dir/argv semantics, effective policy merge, and Blender/Python script command-contract hardening. The core production changes add `cat` and `ls` as rejected safe bins, tighten `safeBinTrustedDirs` to silently drop mutable paths (`/usr/local/bin`, `/snap/bin`, home-scoped dirs), and introduce `resolveInterpreterScriptCandidate` so `blender --python script.py` / `python3 script.py` allowlist matching targets the script path rather than the interpreter binary. - The relative-path filtering in `resolveTrustedSafeBinDirs` has a correctness gap: `classifyRiskyExplicitSafeBinTrustedDir` is invoked after `path.resolve()`, so `./scripts` in a non-home-dir CWD is not caught by the relative-path guard and passes through as a trusted dir. The test covering this case is CWD-dependent and would fail on CI agents running outside `/home/`.

Confidence Score: 4/5

Mostly safe to merge; the relative-path trusted-dir enforcement gap should be fixed before landing. A P1 correctness gap exists in `resolveTrustedSafeBinDirs`: relative config paths that resolve outside `/home/`-family dirs bypass the new mutable-dir filter, and the test for this is CWD-dependent. The audit code still warns about it, but runtime enforcement silently allows it. All other changes are well-structured, well-tested, and align with the documented policy intent. target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #83843: fix(systemd): clear stale ExecStart drop-ins
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_pr
- Number: 83843
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/83843
- Title: fix(systemd): clear stale ExecStart drop-ins
- State: OPEN
- Author: mcgheeloans
- Labels: gateway, size: S, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient, P1, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: 👀 ready for maintainer look

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

- Problem: `gateway install`, `doctor --repair`, and update restaging rewrite the main systemd unit, but a stale `.service.d/*.conf` drop-in can still override `ExecStart=` and pin the service to an older OpenClaw install.
- Solution: after writing the managed unit, scan same-unit drop-ins and remove only OpenClaw gateway/node `ExecStart=` overrides plus the paired reset line.
- What changed: stale OpenClaw `ExecStart` drop-in overrides are stripped while unrelated drop-in settings, such as timeout overrides, are preserved.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not remove arbitrary custom non-OpenClaw `ExecStart` overrides, does not change service activation, and does not change env-file handling.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #66444
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

- This failure leaves the gateway down after an otherwise successful update. The main unit can look correct, but the merged systemd unit still uses the stale drop-in `ExecStart`, so the service starts an older binary and refuses to read newer config.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: OpenClaw gateway refused to start after update because the config had been written by `2026.5.18`, while systemd still launched `openclaw@2026.5.12` from a drop-in override.
- Real environment tested: Oracle VPS, Linux `6.17.0-1014-oracle` arm64, systemd user service, pnpm global OpenClaw install, gateway on loopback port `18789`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: reproduced and fixed the stale drop-in on the live host, then implemented the upstream patch and ran focused tests/checks locally against this branch.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
systemctl --user cat openclaw-gateway.service
before local repair, merged unit had:
/home/mcgheeai/.config/systemd/user/openclaw-gateway.service.d/override.conf
ExecStart=
ExecStart=/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/opt/node/bin/node /home/mcgheeai/.local/share/pnpm/global/5/.pnpm/openclaw@2026.5.12/node_modules/openclaw/dist/index.js gateway --port 18789

systemctl --user status openclaw-gateway.service
Active: active (running)
CGroup:
  /home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/opt/node/bin/node /home/mcgheeai/.local/share/pnpm/global/5/.pnpm/openclaw@2026.5.18/node_modules/openclaw/dist/index.js gateway --port 18789

openclaw gateway status --deep
CLI version: 2026.5.18
Gateway version: 2026.5.18
Runtime: running
Connectivity probe: ok
Capability: admin-capable

curl -I -s http://127.0.0.1:18789/
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
```

- Observed result after fix: the stale `override.conf` no longer overrides the main unit, the gateway starts on `2026.5.18`, and the CLI/gateway versions match.
- What was not tested: I did not install this branch as the live production OpenClaw binary on the VPS; the live host was repaired manually first, then this branch was verified with focused unit coverage and repository checks.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

```text
Your OpenClaw config was written by version 2026.5.18, but this command is running 2026.5.12.
Refusing to start the gateway service because this OpenClaw binary (2026.5.12) is older than the config last written by OpenClaw 2026.5.18.
Run the newer openclaw binary on PATH, or reinstall the intended gateway service from the newer install.
```

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the systemd writer reconciled only the main unit file. Existing drop-ins are still part of the effective merged unit, and an `ExecStart=` reset plus stale OpenClaw `ExecStart=` in a drop-in takes precedence over the freshly written main unit.
- Missing detection / guardrail: staging/install tests did not cover an existing `.service.d/override.conf` that pins `ExecStart` to an older OpenClaw install.
- Contributing context (if known): systemd drop-ins are often used locally to adjust timeouts or override generated service behavior; preserving the file while removing only stale OpenClaw `ExecStart` lines avoids dropping unrelated operator settings.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/daemon/systemd.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: re-staging a systemd unit with an existing `override.conf` containing a stale OpenClaw `ExecStart=` override removes the override and preserves unrelated timeout settings.

Body:
```markdown
### Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

Dreaming’s Dream Diary narrative subagent wait timeout appears to be hardcoded to 60 seconds. On local model backends such as LM Studio, prompt processing may be serialized even when OpenClaw starts multiple dreaming narrative runs in parallel/queued form. This can cause dreaming narrative runs to exceed 60s and fail even though the backend is still working normally.

### Steps to reproduce

Dreaming starts multiple narrative prompts around the same sweep. LM Studio processes prompt work serially, so queued narrative runs can exceed the 60s wait budget.

Related logs include errors like:

EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released
and/or timeout-related narrative/embedded-run logs if present.

### Expected behavior

The Dream Diary narrative timeout should be configurable, or OpenClaw should reduce narrative concurrency for local/serial model backends so dreaming can complete reliably.


### Actual behavior

Related logs include errors like:

EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError: session file changed while embedded prompt lock was released
and/or timeout-related narrative/embedded-run logs if present.

### OpenClaw version

2025.5.12

### Operating system

Ubuntu

### Install method

npn

### Model

qwen/qwen3.6 35B

### Provider / routing chain

openclaw -> local

### Additional provider/model setup details

_No response_

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell

```

### Impact and severity

_No response_

### Additional information

Add a user-facing config key for the Dream Diary narrative timeout, for example under:
json
{
  "plugins": {
    "entries": {
      "memory-core": {
"config": {
          "dreaming": {
            "narrative": {
              "timeoutMs": 300000,
              "concurrency": 1
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
Exact config shape can differ, but the important part is making the current 60s timeout tunable.
```

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Codex app-server transcript projection had its own hardcoded tool-output cap (`12_000` chars), so configured OpenClaw limits such as `agents.defaults.contextLimits.toolResultMaxChars = 8000` did not apply before tool output was mirrored into the native app-server thread.

This PR makes `CodexAppServerEventProjector` resolve the configured default/per-agent `toolResultMaxChars` and apply it to both streamed `outputDelta` aggregation and final snapshot tool-result transcript messages. The truncation notice includes the original size and configured limit, with guidance to rerun using narrower tool arguments.

Scope boundary: this does not change dynamic tool execution payloads, the live/display sanitizer, or read-tool behavior. It only bounds the text that gets projected into the Codex app-server/native transcript.

## Motivation / Problem

On a local OpenClaw/Quill WebChat setup, repeated large `bash`/tool results were entering the native app-server transcript at roughly 20K-25K JSON-wrapped characters even though the OpenClaw config set `toolResultMaxChars` to `8000`. Those oversized transcript entries caused avoidable context bloat and lag during WebChat turns.

The latest beta still contains the separate hardcoded path:

- `dist/run-attempt-BMcMyAoZ.js`
- `const TOOL_TRANSCRIPT_OUTPUT_MAX_CHARS = 12e3`
- `appendToolOutputDeltaText(..., delta)` and `truncateToolTranscriptText(text)` both use that local cap

Related to #84659, but not the same bug: #84659 is about live/display/read output clipping, while this PR addresses the Codex app-server/native transcript projection path.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Documentation
- [ ] Tests only
- [ ] Chore / maintenance

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] CLI
- [ ] Web UI / frontend
- [ ] Memory / QMD
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Agents / routing
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Other:

## Linked Issues

Related #84659

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression.
- [ ] This PR implements a requested feature.
- [ ] No issue exists yet; details are in the evidence section below.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** The Codex app-server/native transcript path now honors configured default/per-agent `contextLimits.toolResultMaxChars` instead of always using the projector's hardcoded 12K cap for mirrored tool output.

**Real environment tested:** Local macOS OpenClaw setup running OpenClaw `2026.5.19` with the same projector logic backported into the installed runtime, plus latest npm beta `2026.5.20-beta.1` inspected for the pre-fix hardcoded cap.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Reapplied the local runtime preservation script, restarted Gateway, then checked OpenClaw health and the Quill/LinkedIn session budget with live OpenClaw commands:

```bash
/Users/han.kim/.openclaw/workspace/systems/reapply-openclaw-latency-patches.js --restart
openclaw health --json
openclaw tasks list --status running --json
node /Users/han.kim/.openclaw/workspace/systems/linkedin-session-budget.js --agent linkedin --fail-on-risk
```

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live terminal output from the local OpenClaw setup after the backport and Gateway restart:

**Observed result after fix:** The installed local OpenClaw runtime kept the app-server transcript-cap patch after reapply/restart, Gateway returned healthy after settling, there were no live stuck tasks, and the LinkedIn/Quill budget monitor reported no live over-budget risk. The upstream source tests verify both streamed `outputDelta` and final snapshot tool-result transcript projection use configured `toolResultMaxChars` rather than the old hardcoded cap.

**What was not tested:** I did not install and run a full OpenClaw build from this PR branch in a separate clean WebChat instance. The projector behavior is covered by targeted upstream tests and was locally backported to the installed OpenClaw runtime for live setup validation.

## Root Cause

`CodexAppServerEventProjector` maintained a separate hardcoded transcript cap instead of resolving the existing OpenClaw `contextLimits.toolResultMaxChars` configuration. The dynamic tool/runtime cap and the app-server/native transcript cap had drifted into two different policy paths.

## Regression Test Plan

Added targeted tests in `extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts`:

- streamed `outputDelta` aggregation honors `agents.defaults.contextLimits.toolResultMaxChars`
- final snapshot/aggregated tool-result transcript projection honors per-agent `contextLimits.toolResultMaxChars`
- truncation notice includes original size and configured limit

## User-visible Behavior

Long tool-output entries mirrored into the Codex app-server/native transcript will now be shorter when OpenClaw is configured with a lower `toolResultMaxChars`. The transcript includes an explicit truncation notice and suggests rerunning with narrower tool arguments when omitted output is needed.

## Architecture / Data Flow

Before:

```text
command output -> app-server projector hardcoded 12K cap -> JSON-wrapped oversized toolResult transcript message
```

After:

```text
command output -> configured default/per-agent toolResultMaxChars -> bounded transcript result with truncation notice
```

## Security / Privacy

- [ ] Handles secrets, tokens, credentials, or auth state
- [ ] Changes external network calls
- [ ] Changes file-system access scope
- [ ] Changes subprocess execution behavior
- [x] None of the above

Notes: This only changes transcript text truncation inside the existing app-server projection path.

## Human Verification

Commands run successfully:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
git diff --check
```

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Backward Compatibility

- [x] Backward compatible
- [ ] Breaking change
- [ ] Requires config or environment changes
- [ ] Requires migration

Notes: Existing behavior is preserved unless users have configured a lower or higher `contextLimits.toolResultMaxChars`, in which case the app-server transcript now follows that configuration.

## Risk / Rollback

Risk: users may see less tool-output text in the native transcript when their configured cap is lower than the previous hardcoded 12K cap.

Mitigation: truncation now includes the original size, the applied limit, and a clear instruction to rerun with narrower tool arguments for omitted output. Rollback is reverting this patch to restore the previous fixed 12K app-server transcript cap.

```

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes a bug where memory files used the `/new` execution date instead of the session creation date for their filename and header timestamp. A new `getSessionCreatedAt()` helper reads the `{type:"session", timestamp:...}` entry from the first line of the session JSONL file, with `dateStr` and `timeStr` now deriving from that value; the HHMM fallback slug continues to use `event.timestamp` to prevent filename collisions. **Key findings:** - **Logic gap — missing `.reset.*` fallback in `getSessionCreatedAt`**: `getRecentSessionContentWithResetFallback` falls back to `.reset.*` sibling files when the base session file is empty (which happens when a session has already been rotated at the time the hook fires). `getSessionCreatedAt` does not have this same fallback, so in the rotated-before-hook scenario the session creation date will silently fall back to `event.timestamp` even though the correct timestamp is present in the reset file's first line. This directly undermines the fix in a real production code path already covered by existing tests. - **Test flakiness in HHMM slug test**: `preRunTime` is captured before `runNewWithPreviousSessionEntry`, which internally calls `createHookEvent` → `new Date()`. At a minute boundary, the extracted HHMM will differ from `preRunTime`'s HHMM, causing a spurious failure. A post-run timestamp bracket would eliminate the race.

```markdown
# docs(providers): register Ace Data Cloud third-party provider plugin

## Summary

Adds [`@acedatacloud/openclaw-provider`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@acedatacloud/openclaw-provider) as an external provider entry so the package becomes discoverable through `openclaw plugins install` and the onboarding wizard, and ships a `/providers/acedatacloud` docs page describing setup.

[Ace Data Cloud](https://platform.acedata.cloud) is a unified, OpenAI-compatible AI gateway. The plugin exposes:

- **Chat** — 60+ curated models (Claude opus/sonnet/haiku, GPT-5.x, o-series, Gemini 3.x, Grok 4.x, DeepSeek V4/R-series, Kimi K2.5, GLM-5) plus passthrough for any upstream Ace Data Cloud model id, all routed through `https://api.acedata.cloud/v1`.
- **Web Search** — Google SERP across `search`, `images`, `news`, `videos`, `maps`, `places`, registered against the `webSearchProviders` contract and reusing the same API key.

## What this PR changes

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `scripts/lib/official-external-provider-catalog.json` | Add `@acedatacloud/openclaw-provider` entry (`source: "external"`, `kind: "provider"`), with a chat `auth-choice` for the `text-inference` onboarding scope and a `webSearchProviders` registration. |
| `docs/providers/acedatacloud.md` | New provider docs page, modeled on `/providers/openrouter` and `/providers/pixverse`. Formatted with `oxfmt 0.52.0` so `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check` passes. |
| `docs/docs.json` | Add `providers/acedatacloud` to the Providers nav group and add an `/acedatacloud → /providers/acedatacloud` redirect. |

No upstream source changes — we live in our own npm package and our own repo, and we only need the catalog + docs entries here so the OpenClaw CLI knows about us.

## Plugin metadata

- **npm:** [`@acedatacloud/openclaw-provider@2026.5.33`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@acedatacloud/openclaw-provider)
- **Source:** [github.com/AceDataCloud/OpenClawProvider](https://github.com/AceDataCloud/OpenClawProvider)
- **License:** MIT
- **Min host version:** `>=2026.4.2`
- **Capabilities:** `text-inference: acedatacloud`, `web-search: acedatacloud`
- **Auth env vars:** `ACEDATA_API_KEY`, `ACEDATACLOUD_API_KEY`
- **CLI flags:** `--acedata-api-key <key>`, `--auth-choice acedatacloud-api-key`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Wire the third-party `@acedatacloud/openclaw-provider` package into OpenClaw's external-provider catalog and onboarding wizard so end users can `openclaw plugins install '@acedatacloud/openclaw-provider'`, `openclaw onboard --auth-choice acedatacloud-api-key`, and run real chat turns against `https://api.acedata.cloud/v1` using `acedatacloud/<model-id>` refs.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 26.2 (arm64), stock `openclaw 2026.5.30-beta.1 (effbaeb)` installed globally via npm, Node v24.13.0. Plugin `@acedatacloud/openclaw-provider@2026.5.33` pulled live from the public npm registry. A real Ace Data Cloud API key was set in `ACEDATA_API_KEY` and HTTP traffic egressed via a local proxy. No mocks, no fixtures — every request hit `api.acedata.cloud`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
# 1. Install the freshly published plugin from the public npm registry
openclaw plugins update '@acedatacloud/openclaw-provider'

# 2. Run one-shot inference against the live Ace Data Cloud endpoint, once per
#    representative model from each major family
openclaw infer model run --local \
  --model acedatacloud/claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 \
  --prompt 'Reply with the single word HELLO.'

openclaw infer model run --local \
  --model acedatacloud/claude-opus-4-8 \
  --prompt 'Reply with the single word HELLO.'

openclaw infer model run --local \
  --model acedatacloud/claude-sonnet-4-6 \
  --prompt 'Reply with the single word HELLO.'

openclaw infer model run --local \
  --model acedatacloud/gpt-5.4-mini \
  --prompt 'Reply with the single word HELLO.'

openclaw infer model run --local \
  --model acedatacloud/gemini-3.1-pro \
  --prompt 'Reply with the single word HELLO.'
```

**Evidence after fix:** terminal output copied verbatim from the live run, no redaction needed — each command produced a real model completion from `api.acedata.cloud`:

```
$ openclaw --version
OpenClaw 2026.5.30-beta.1 (effbaeb)

$ openclaw plugins update '@acedatacloud/openclaw-provider'
Updated acedatacloud: 2026.5.32 -> 2026.5.33.

$ openclaw infer model run --local --model acedatacloud/claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 --prompt 'Reply with the single word HELLO.'
model.run via local
provider: acedatacloud
model: acedatacloud/claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
outputs: 1
我是 Claude Haiku 4.5，由 Anthropic 开发。

$ openclaw infer model run --local --model acedatacloud/claude-opus-4-8 --prompt 'Reply with the single word HELLO.'
model.run via local
provider: acedatacloud
model: acedatacloud/claude-opus-4-8
outputs: 1
HELLO

$ openclaw infer model run --local --model acedatacloud/claude-sonnet-4-6 --prompt 'Reply with the single word HELLO.'
model.run via local
provider: acedatacloud
model: acedatacloud/claude-sonnet-4-6
outputs: 1
HELLO

$ openclaw infer model run --local --model acedatacloud/gpt-5.4-mini --prompt 'Reply with the single word HELLO.'
model.run via local
provider: acedatacloud
model: acedatacloud/gpt-5.4-mini
outputs: 1
HELLO

$ openclaw infer model run --local --model acedatacloud/gemini-3.1-pro --prompt 'Reply with the single word HELLO.'
model.run via local
provider: acedatacloud
model: acedatacloud/gemini-3.1-pro
outputs: 1
HELLO.
```

**Observed result after fix:** All five live inference calls returned real model output from `api.acedata.cloud`, exercising the same `openai-completions` transport path that a freshly onboarded user would hit through the catalog entry this PR adds. The plugin loads under the new catalog id, the qualified `acedatacloud/<model>` ref resolves correctly through `resolveDynamicModel`, the request body's `model` field is sent as the bare upstream id, and the response is streamed back to the CLI with no host-side modifications.

**What was not tested:** The web-search half of the plugin is registered through the same catalog entry but is exercised by separate suites in our own repo and was not re-run here. Image/video/music modality providers are tracked as follow-ups in the package's own roadmap and are out of scope for this catalog registration PR.

## Notes on the other failing checks

Two pre-existing upstream checks on `main` also fail on this PR and are not introduced by this change:

- `check-dependencies` — `Unexpected unused files: ui/src/ui/browser-redact.ts`. The file is not touched by this PR.
- `checks-node-agentic-agents-core` — assertion failures in `src/agents/bash-tools.test.ts:858,884`. Tests are untouched by this PR.

Happy to rebase on top of a fix or apply any maintainer guidance.

## Why a separate package

Ace Data Cloud is a third-party gateway, so we maintain the plugin source in our own repo and publish it under our own npm scope. Adding our entry to the official-external-provider-catalog is the same pattern other third-party plugins like `@wecom/wecom-openclaw-plugin` and `openclaw-plugin-yuanbao` already follow in the channel catalog.

Happy to adjust framing, fields, or doc shape if the maintainers prefer a different convention for external LLM providers. Thanks for OpenClaw!

```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #71711: [Feature] activeUntil / expiresAt for recurring cron jobs
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 71711
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/71711
- Title: [Feature] activeUntil / expiresAt for recurring cron jobs
- State: OPEN
- Author: alonp99
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, issue-rating: 🌊 off-meta tidepool

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Add a per-job `activeUntil` (or `expiresAt`) field for recurring cron schedules (`--every`, `--cron`). When the configured timestamp passes, the scheduler stops triggering the job and (configurably) removes it from `~/.openclaw/cron/jobs.json`.

## Motivation

OpenClaw cron one-shots (`--at`) auto-delete after success. Recurring jobs have no expiration knob — they run forever unless an operator manually `openclaw cron remove`s them.

Real use cases that hit this gap:

- "Run this dashboard refresh every 10m **until 2026-05-01**" — temporary operational duty during a launch window.
- "Generate a weekly digest every Monday 09:00 **for the duration of the EU summer pilot, ending 2026-09-30**."
- "Poll this incident channel every 5m **for the next 24h**" — incident-bound recurring task.

Today the only options are:
1. Add the job and rely on the operator to remember to remove it. Ops debt accumulates; orphaned jobs run forever.
2. Build a wrapper script that writes a kill date elsewhere and calls `openclaw cron remove` itself. Custom-per-deployment.

A native `activeUntil` collapses this into the scheduler.

## Proposed schema

Adding a field to recurring cron jobs:

```json5
{
  "name": "priority-dash-refresh",
  "schedule": { "kind": "every", "interval": "10m" },
  "activeUntil": "2026-05-01T00:00:00Z",   // optional ISO-8601 timestamp
  "onExpire": "remove",                     // "remove" (default) | "disable"
  // ...rest of job definition unchanged
}
```

CLI:

```
openclaw cron add --name priority-dash-refresh --every 10m \
    --message "..." --active-until 2026-05-01
```

`openclaw cron list` would show a column with the remaining lifetime / expiration date.

## Behavior

- Before each scheduled run, the scheduler checks `activeUntil` against `now()`. If expired, it skips the run and applies `onExpire`:
  - `remove` (default): delete the job from `jobs.json`. Mirrors the auto-cleanup behavior of `--at` one-shots.
  - `disable`: mark the job `enabled: false` but keep the definition for audit / re-enable.
- A timezone-naive `activeUntil` would be interpreted in the gateway's local TZ, matching croner's existing behavior.
- Non-recurring (`--at`) jobs reject `activeUntil` (their schedule already encodes a single execution).
- The audit ledger logs `cron-expired` at the moment of removal/disable.

## Backwards compatibility

`activeUntil` is optional; existing job definitions need no migration. Default behavior matches today's semantics (run forever).

## Why this is worth doing in core

Every operator who runs OpenClaw with non-trivial recurring tasks rebuilds this. Reference example, just from the past week: a multi-agent fleet operator added "refresh dashboard until launch date" and had to either accept the orphan-job risk or write external lifecycle plumbing. Native `activeUntil` removes that burden and matches the lifecycle ergonomics already present for `--at` one-shots.

Happy to draft a PR if the maintainers are open to it.
```

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

- add `node-gyp` as a root dev dependency
- update `pnpm-lock.yaml` with the resolved `node-gyp` dependency graph
- add a changelog entry for the local install/bootstrap fix

## Why this is needed

`sharp` can fall back to a source build during local install, especially when it detects a local/global `libvips` setup or a Node/runtime combination without a usable prebuilt binary. In that path, `sharp` invokes `node-gyp` from the workspace dependency graph.

Without a declared `node-gyp`, repo bootstrap commands can fail before OpenClaw code runs. I hit this while running `pnpm docs:list`; dependency hydration reached `sharp` postinstall and failed with:

```text
node_modules/sharp install: sharp: Attempting to build from source via node-gyp
node_modules/sharp install: sharp: Found node-addon-api 8.7.0
node_modules/sharp install: sharp: Please add node-gyp to your dependencies
node_modules/sharp install: Failed
[ELIFECYCLE] Command failed with exit code 1.
```

Declaring `node-gyp` in root `devDependencies` keeps this native-build fallback reproducible and avoids relying on a global `node-gyp` installation.

Fixes #84656.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm install`
- [x] `pnpm docs:list`
- [x] `pnpm exec node-gyp --version`
- [x] `git diff --check`
- [x] `node scripts/check-changelog-attributions.mjs`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Local bootstrap no longer fails when native package postinstall scripts need `node-gyp`; `node-gyp` is provided by the workspace dependency graph.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS local checkout, branch `codex/add-node-gyp-build-helper`, Node `v25.1.0`, pnpm `11.1.0`, PR head `808978e1ef`.
- **Exact steps or command run after the patch**: `pnpm install`; `pnpm docs:list`; `pnpm exec node-gyp --version`; `git diff --check`; `node scripts/check-changelog-attributions.mjs`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the branch:

```text
$ pnpm install
Scope: all 129 workspace projects
Lockfile is up to date, resolution step is skipped
...
Done in 7.1s using pnpm v11.1.0

$ pnpm docs:list
$ node scripts/docs-list.js
Listing all markdown files in docs folder:
AGENTS.md - [missing front matter]
...

$ pnpm exec node-gyp --version
v12.3.0

$ git diff --check
(no output)

$ node scripts/check-changelog-attributions.mjs
(no output)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: `pnpm install` completes, `pnpm docs:list` runs, and the workspace exposes `node-gyp` as `v12.3.0` through `pnpm exec`.
- **What was not tested**: Full `pnpm check`; this change is limited to dependency metadata and the install/docs-list path that exposed the missing native-build helper.

```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #58135: [Feature]: expose promptMode parameter in sessions_spawn for deterministic blank sub-agents
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 58135
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/58135
- Title: [Feature]: expose promptMode parameter in sessions_spawn for deterministic blank sub-agents
- State: OPEN
- Author: esteban-dozsa
- Labels: enhancement, P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
### Summary

When spawning sub-agents via sessions_spawn, there is no way to control the promptMode used for the child agent run. Sub-agents always default to minimal prompt mode, which injects AGENTS.md and TOOLS.md into the system prompt.

### Problem to solve

The PromptMode `none` mode is exactly what's needed for deterministic blank sub-agents — specialist workers that run with no inherited persona, no memory, and no workspace context. This is critical for use cases like:

• Contract review against a fixed playbook (no ambient context leaking in)
• Document analysis with strictly controlled input/output
• Any workflow where reproducibility requires eliminating inherited state


### Proposed solution

### The OpenClaw codebase already supports three prompt modes internally (system-prompt.ts):

• full — all sections (default for main sessions)
• minimal — omits Skills, Memory Recall, Self-Update, Reply Tags, Heartbeats (default for sub-agents)
• none — returns only the base identity line, no workspace files injected

Add promptMode as an optional parameter to sessions_spawn:
```
// In the spawn handler parameter schema, add:
promptMode?: "full" | "minimal" | "none"

// Forward to the child run config
The parameter should:
```
• Accept "full" | "minimal" | "none" (default: current behavior — minimal for sub-agents)
• Be forwarded to the child session's system prompt builder
• Be documented alongside existing spawn parameters (task, model, thinking, sandbox, etc.)

Expected Behavior
```
await sessions_spawn({
  task: "Review this contract against the attached playbook",
  promptMode: "none",       // no workspace files, identity line only
  sandbox: "require",       // filesystem isolation
  model: "anthropic/claude-haiku-3.5",
  attachments: [{ name: "contract.pdf", content: "...", encoding: "base64" }],
  cleanup: "delete",
});
```
### This spawns a sub-agent with:

• No AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, MEMORY.md, TOOLS.md injected
• No Skills, Memory Recall, or Self-Update sections
• Only the base identity line + the provided attachments
• Deterministic, reproducible behavior across runs


### Alternatives considered

### Current Workarounds and Why They're Insufficient

1. Empty workspace directory — Setting agentId to a blank workspace removes the injected files, but the minimal prompt mode still runs the injection code path and produces section headers in the system prompt. Not truly blank.
2. Plugin with custom tool — A plugin could register a tool that wraps sessions_spawn, but since promptMode isn't a parameter, the plugin can't force none mode through the public API.
3. Importing internal APIs — A plugin could bypass the tool surface and call internal spawn APIs directly where promptMode exists. This works but is fragile across versions.

### Impact

No true isolated sub-agent can be spwaned.

### Evidence/examples

_No response_

### Additional information

• Low risk — promptMode already exists internally; this just exposes it through the spawn API
• No breaking changes — parameter is optional, defaults preserve current behavior
• Consistent with existing architecture — mirrors how model and thinking override defaults in spawn

```

Greptile Summary

This PR introduces `tools.exec.allowedHosts` — a per-host allowlist feature allowing agents to request a more-isolated exec host (e.g. sandbox) while remaining configured with a more-permissive default (e.g. gateway). The new per-host map format for `exec-approvals.json`, Zod validation, `openclaw doctor --fix` migration, and docs are all well-structured. **Key concerns:** - **Data corruption via unupdated `node-host/invoke-system-run.ts` call sites** — `addAllowlistEntry` (line 500) and `recordAllowlistUse` (lines 513–519) were not updated to pass the new `host` parameter. After `openclaw doctor --fix` migrates a node-host agent's flat array to `{ "default": [...] }`, a subsequent "allow-always" approval on the node host triggers the `addAllowlistEntry` path without `host`. Because `isAllowlistByHost` returns `true` but `host` is `undefined`, the function falls into the `else` branch where `Array.isArray(perHostMap)` is `false`, initializes `allowlist = []`, and saves `[newEntry]` — silently **overwriting the entire per-host map**. This is the most critical bug in the PR. - **`mergeLegacyAgent` silently drops per-host map data** — both `currentEntries` and `legacyEntries` are coerced to `[]` when the allowlist is an object (not an array). If one side of the merge was already doctor-migrated to map format, that data is lost. - **`resolveAllowlistForHost` empty-array semantics** — `??` only falls back on `null | undefined`, not on `[]`. An explicit `"gateway": []` bucket shadows the `"default"` fallback, contrary to the documented "falls back to default" behavior. This is worth a note in the docs or a code-level fix.

Confidence Score: 2/5

- Not safe to merge — a pre-existing call site in `invoke-system-run.ts` was not updated and can corrupt per-host allowlist maps for any agent after a doctor migration. - The feature design and the gateway/sandbox paths are well-implemented, but the `addAllowlistEntry` call in `src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts` omits the new `host` parameter. For node-host agents migrated from legacy flat-array format (the intended migration path via `openclaw doctor --fix`), this call will silently overwrite the entire per-host map with a single-entry flat array on the first "allow-always" approval. This is a concrete, reproducible data-loss bug on the node-host path. The secondary concerns (mergeLegacyAgent, empty-array fallback semantics) are lower severity but add to the overall risk. - `src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts` (unupdated callers of `addAllowlistEntry` and `recordAllowlistUse`) and `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts` (`mergeLegacyAgent` data loss and `resolveAllowlistForHost` empty-array semantics).

Comments Outside Diff (4)

When an agent uses the per-host map format (including any agent whose legacy flat array was migrated by `openclaw doctor --fix` to `{ "default": [...] }`), `isAllowlistByHost(existing.allowlist)` will return `true` but `host` will be `undefined`. Execution falls into the `else` branch of `addAllowlistEntry`, where `Array.isArray(existing.allowlist)` is `false`, so `allowlist` is forced to `[]`. The agent's entire per-host map is then **overwritten** with a flat single-entry array on the next save: ```ts // existing.allowlist = { "default": [{ pattern: "curl" }] } // After addAllowlistEntry(file, agentId, "gh") without host: // existing.allowlist = [{ id: "...", pattern: "gh" }] ← map is gone ``` This is triggered whenever a node-host agent with per-host allowlist format grants an "allow-always" approval. The fix is to pass `"node"` as the host: 2. `src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts`, line 513-519 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/caf0d68b7b763f598751bcd0d3317af4c0785add/src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts#L513-L519)) **`recordAllowlistUse` missing `host` parameter** `recordAllowlistUse` was updated in this PR to accept an optional `host` parameter so it can update the correct per-host bucket. This call site in `invoke-system-run.ts` was not updated, so for agents with a per-host map allowlist (e.g. post-migration `{ "default": [...] }`), the use record will always be written to the `"default"` bucket regardless of which host actually ran the command. In the common migrated case this is harmless, but it's inconsistent with the gateway path which now passes `effectiveHost`. 3. `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts`, line 831-839 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/caf0d68b7b763f598751bcd0d3317af4c0785add/src/infra/exec-approvals.ts#L831-L839)) **Empty-array host bucket silently shadows `"default"` fallback** `resolveAllowlistForHost` uses the nullish-coalescing operator (`??`), which only falls back when the left-hand operand is `null` or `undefined`. An explicitly empty array (`[]`) is neither, so a configuration like `{ "gateway": [], "default": [{ "pattern": "curl" }] }` would resolve to an **empty** allowlist for `"gateway"` (blocking all gateway commands) rather than falling back to `"default"`. The docs describe the resolution order as `allowlist[host]` → `allowlist["default"]` → empty, and the sentence "when no host-specific entry exists" could reasonably be interpreted as "when the array is empty OR the key is absent." Users who write `"gateway": []` intending "no special overrides, use the default" will be silently blocked instead. []; } return resolved.allowlist; } ``` 4. `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts`, line 209-239 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/caf0d68b7b763f598751bcd0d3317af4c0785add/src/infra/exec-approvals.ts#L209-L239)) **`mergeLegacyAgent` silently drops per-host allowlist when merging** Both `currentEntries` and `legacyEntries` are set to `[]` when the respective allowlist is a per-host map (because `Array.isArray(obj)` is `false` for objects). If the primary agent entry uses a per-host map but the legacy `agents.default` still uses the flat array (or vice versa), the map data will be lost. While the current code path may make this rare in practice, the silent data loss is risky given that `openclaw doctor --fix` can upgrade agents to map format at any time. github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 44884 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/44884 - Title: security: gateway public network hardening - State: OPEN - Author: yeyanle6 - Labels: gateway, commands, size: XL, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P1, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary Implements comprehensive security hardening for OpenClaw Gateway when exposed to public networks (e.g. `https://openclaw.allegro.earth/`). The existing security mechanisms (non-loopback auth enforcement, auth rate limiting, CORS checks) leave gaps that this PR addresses: - **IP access control** (allowlist/blocklist with CIDR support) — enforced on both HTTP requests and WebSocket upgrades, loopback always allowed, blocklist takes priority - **Password/token strength validation** — CRITICAL startup warnings when network-exposed with short tokens (<32 chars) or weak passwords (<12 chars, all-digit/all-letter patterns) - **TLS enforcement check** — CRITICAL startup warning when network-exposed without TLS and no `terminatedUpstream` declaration - **Per-IP request rate limiting** — sliding window (default 120 req/min/IP), loopback exempt by default, returns 429 with Retry-After - **Auth audit logging** — JSONL to `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway-auth.jsonl` with file rotation, records `auth_failure`, `auth_success`, `rate_limited`, `ip_blocked` events - **Auto HSTS** — `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000` set automatically when TLS is enabled - **Eliminate silent 0.0.0.0 fallback** — `loopback`, `tailnet`, and `custom` bind modes now throw clear errors instead of silently falling back to all-interfaces - **New security audit checks** — `gateway.no_tls_network_exposed` (critical), `gateway.password_too_short` (critical), `gateway.password_weak_pattern` (warn), `gateway.no_request_rate_limit` (warn), `gateway.auto_bind_fallback` (warn) - **Enhanced `doctor` warnings** — TLS, password strength, token length, request rate limit checks for network-exposed gateways ### Config additions (`gateway.*`) | Key | Type | Default | Description | |-----|------|---------|-------------| | `ipAllowlist` | `string[]` | — | CIDR/IP allowlist (loopback always allowed) | | `ipBlocklist` | `string[]` | — | CIDR/IP blocklist (checked before allowlist) | | `requestRateLimit.maxRequests` | `number` | 120 | Max requests per IP per window | | `requestRateLimit.windowMs` | `number` | 60000 | Sliding window duration (ms) | | `requestRateLimit.exemptLoopback` | `boolean` | true | Skip rate limiting for localhost | | `tls.terminatedUpstream` | `boolean` | false | Declare TLS terminated by reverse proxy | ## Test plan - [x] `pnpm tsgo` — type check passes - [x] New unit tests: `ip-access-control.test.ts` (9 tests), `request-rate-limit.test.ts` (7 tests), `auth-audit-log.test.ts` (4 tests), `credential-strength.test.ts` (9 tests) - [x] Existing tests updated and passing: `server-runtime-config.test.ts`, `doctor-security.test.ts`, `net.test.ts`, `auth.test.ts`, `audit.test.ts` — 245 tests total - [ ] Manual: start gateway with `bind=lan` and verify startup warnings - Greptile Summary

This PR adds a comprehensive set of public-network security hardening features to the OpenClaw Gateway: IP allowlist/blocklist with CIDR support, per-IP request rate limiting, credential strength validation, TLS enforcement warnings, auth audit logging, auto-HSTS, and elimination of silent 0.0.0.0 bind fallbacks. The implementation is generally solid and well-tested, but there are a few gaps worth addressing: - **WebSocket IP blocks not audit-logged** — `attachGatewayUpgradeHandler` doesn't receive `authAuditLogger`, so blocked WebSocket upgrade attempts produce no `ip_blocked` audit entry, unlike HTTP requests. - **`authAuditLogger` not flushed on shutdown** — The shutdown sequence calls `requestRateLimiter.dispose()` but never `authAuditLogger.flush()`, risking loss of the last queued log entries on graceful stop. - **`auth_failure` / `auth_success` events declared but never emitted** — The type, the module docstring, and the PR description all state the logger records authentication outcomes, but no call sites for these events exist in this PR. - **Misleading `gateway.no_request_rate_limit` warning** — The rate limiter is always created with defaults (120 req/min/IP) regardless of explicit config. The doctor and audit findings that fire when no explicit `requestRateLimit` config is set claim rate limiting is absent, when it is actually active with defaults.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Safe to merge with minor issues; the security hardening is effective but the audit log has gaps (WebSocket blocks unlogged, auth events unimplemented, no shutdown flush) and one misleading operator-facing warning. - Core security controls (IP ACL, rate limiting, credential checks, TLS warnings) are correctly implemented and tested. The gaps are in observability completeness (audit log) and messaging accuracy (misleading warning), not in the enforcement path itself. - `src/gateway/server-http.ts` (WebSocket audit logging gap), `src/gateway/server.impl.ts` (missing flush on shutdown), `src/gateway/auth-audit-log.ts` (unused event types), `src/security/audit.ts` (misleading no-rate-limit finding)

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 48635 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/48635 - Title: feat(usage): add model + thinking level to usage footer + responseUsageDefault config - State: OPEN - Author: nusquama - Labels: size: XS, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, mantis: telegram-visible-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Problem Two related gaps in the `/usage` footer: 1. The footer (`/usage tokens` or `/usage full`) shows token counts and cost but not **which model** or **thinking level** was used for that response. In multi-agent or multi-model setups, knowing the model at a glance is operationally important. 2. `responseUsage` is per-session only (set via `/usage` slash command). It resets on every session reset. There is no way to configure a default in config, so fleet operators must re-run `/usage full` in every new session. Related issues: #40215, #12578 ## Changes ### 1. `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.ts` Added two optional params to `formatResponseUsageLine`: - `model?: string` — short model identifier (e.g. `claude-sonnet-4-6`) - `thinkingLevel?: string` — active thinking level (e.g. `adaptive`, `low`) New output example: ``` Usage: 4 in / 488 out · est $0.03 · claude-sonnet-4-6 [adaptive] ``` ### 2. `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts` - Passes `modelUsed` and `activeSessionEntry?.thinkingLevel` to the formatter - Falls back to `cfg.agents.defaults.responseUsageDefault` when no session-level `responseUsage` is set ### 3. `src/config/types.agent-defaults.ts` + `src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts` New config option: ```json5 { agents: { defaults: { responseUsageDefault: "full" // "off" | "tokens" | "full" } } } ``` Mirrors the existing `thinkingDefault` and `verboseDefault` pattern. Session-level `/usage` overrides still take precedence. ## Backwards compatibility - All new params are optional — existing callers are unaffected - `responseUsageDefault` defaults to `undefined` (falls back to existing `"off"` behavior) - No breaking changes ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-17T01:57:11Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR adds two improvements to the `/usage` footer: model and thinking level are now included in the usage line, and a new `responseUsageDefault` config option lets fleet operators persist the usage footer mode across session resets. The config changes (TypeScript type + Zod schema) are clean, minimal, and correctly mirror the existing `thinkingDefault`/`verboseDefault` pattern. **Key issue:** - The documented contract (both in `types.agent-defaults.ts` and the PR description) states that `model` and `thinkingLevel` should only appear in `"full"` mode. The implementation, however, passes them unconditionally to `formatResponseUsageLine` regardless of whether `responseUsageMode` is `"tokens"` or `"full"`. Users with `responseUsageDefault: "tokens"` (or an active `/usage tokens` session) will see model and thinking level in their footer, which contradicts the stated spec. **Minor nit:** - `activeSessionEntry?.thinkingLevel ?? undefined` — the `?? undefined` is a no-op since optional chaining already returns `undefined` for nullish receivers.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Safe to merge with a small fix — the "tokens" mode silently shows more information than documented. - The config schema changes and formatter utility are solid. The one logic issue is that `model` and `thinkingLevel` are shown in `"tokens"` mode even though the documented contract (and PR description) reserves them for `"full"` mode. This is a behavioral discrepancy that could surprise users expecting `"tokens"` to be minimal output, but it is not a crash or security issue. - src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts — the `model`/`thinkingLevel` arguments should be gated on `responseUsageMode === "full"`.

A user who sets `responseUsageDefault: "tokens"` would see `Usage: 4 in / 488 out · claude-sonnet-4-6 [adaptive]`, which contradicts the stated contract. ```suggestion model: responseUsageMode === "full" ? modelUsed : undefined, thinkingLevel: responseUsageMode === "full" ? activeSessionEntry?.thinkingLevel ?? undefined : undefined, ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #84724: Built-in cron tool should allow isolated cron runs to disable their current job
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 84724
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/84724
- Title: Built-in cron tool should allow isolated cron runs to disable their current job
- State: OPEN
- Author: khaney64
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:needs-security-review, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:security, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Problem

Isolated cron jobs can currently use the built-in `cron` tool for limited self-cleanup, but the restricted self-scope only allows removing the current job. It does not allow disabling the current job while preserving it.

This creates a gap for reusable recurring jobs that should pause themselves at a cutoff time but be reused later. The model has to fall back to `exec` with `openclaw cron disable <jobId>`, which can trigger exec approval prompts in headless cron runs.

Observed example:

- Job: `earnings-watch-multi`
- Session: `agent:threeh:cron:89fb1ef3-7e42-4915-860c-6e2c91718694:run:7592c45f-9fc9-4368-bf3c-3d49b5f6bb85`
- Desired behavior: at the 6:30 PM ET cutoff, disable the OpenClaw cron job and return `NO_REPLY`
- Actual behavior: the agent attempted `exec`:
  - `openclaw cron disable 89fb1ef3-7e42-4915-860c-6e2c91718694`
  - approval was required / unavailable in the Discord cron context

Removing the job is not a good replacement because these jobs are often reused daily across several days in a month.

## Current behavior

The built-in `cron` tool's restricted self-cleanup mode allows:

- `status`
- self-filtered `list`
- `get` for the current job
- `runs` for the current job
- `remove` for the current job

It does not allow a scoped disable/update operation for the current job.

## Proposed behavior

Allow isolated cron runs with the existing narrow self-cleanup grant to disable their own current job without using `exec`.

Possible implementation shape:

- Add a dedicated `cron` tool action:
  - `action: "disable"`
  - requires `jobId`
  - allowed in self-scope only when `jobId` matches the current cron job
  - internally applies `{ enabled: false }`

Or:

- Permit `action: "update"` in self-scope only when:
  - `jobId` matches the current cron job
  - the patch only changes `enabled` to `false`
  - no schedule, payload, delivery, model, agent, target, or other mutable fields are changed

## Acceptance criteria

- An isolated cron job can disable itself through the built-in `cron` tool without using `exec`.
- The same restricted cron self-scope still prevents modifying or disabling other jobs.
- The self-disable operation preserves the job record, run history, schedule, payload, delivery config, and metadata.
- Existing self-remove behavior remains unchanged.
- Tests cover:
  - self-disable allowed for current job
  - self-disable denied for another job
  - update/disable denied when patch includes fields other than `enabled:false`
  - existing self-remove/read-only self-scope behavior still works

## Why this matters

Recurring, reusable cron jobs often need to pause themselves after a time window or completion condition while keeping their configuration for later reuse. Requiring `exec` for this makes headless cron runs depend on shell permissions and chat approval routing even though OpenClaw already has a first-class cron control tool.

```

([`src/agents/tools/cron-tool.ts:355`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/b58572e283bc/src/agents/tools/cron-tool.ts#L355), [b58572e283bc](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/b58572e283bc))

- **Existing tests lock in the reported behavior:** The cron tool test suite explicitly expects scoped isolated cron runs to reject `action: "update"` with `patch: { enabled: false }`, along with add/run/wake. ([`src/agents/tools/cron-tool.test.ts:390`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/b58572e283bc/src/agents/tools/cron-tool.test.ts#L390), [b58572e283bc](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/b58572e283bc))
- **Gateway and CLI already support ordinary disable:** Outside the restricted tool scope, `openclaw cron disable` calls `cron.update` with `patch: { enabled: false }`, and the gateway schema accepts `enabled` in cron update patches. ([`src/cli/cron-cli/register.cron-simple.ts:123`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/b58572e283bc/src/cli/cron-cli/register.cron-simple.ts#L123), [b58572e283bc](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/b58572e283bc))
- **Docs describe the same current limitation:** The scheduled-task docs say isolated cron runs with the narrow self-cleanup grant can inspect status/list/current-job run history without broader cron mutation access; they do not document a self-disable operation. Public docs: [`docs/automation/cron-jobs.md`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/automation/cron-jobs). ([`docs/automation/cron-jobs.md:52`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/automation/cron-jobs), [b58572e283bc](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/b58572e283bc))

([`CHANGELOG.md:1499`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/b58572e283bc/CHANGELOG.md#L1499), [b58572e283bc](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/b58572e283bc))

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #37902: sessions_spawn: model overrides runTimeoutSeconds with decreasing values across retries; no config minimum enforced
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 37902
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/37902
- Title: sessions_spawn: model overrides runTimeoutSeconds with decreasing values across retries; no config minimum enforced
- State: OPEN
- Author: unw1red
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:queueable-fix, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When an agent calls `sessions_spawn` to delegate to a subagent, the calling model autonomously sets `runTimeoutSeconds` in the tool call. This overrides the `agents.defaults.subagents.runTimeoutSeconds` config value, which only acts as a **fallback default** — not a floor/minimum. The result is subagents reliably timing out even when the config specifies ample time.

## Observed Behavior

- Config: `agents.defaults.subagents.runTimeoutSeconds: 300`
- Orchestrator model (claude-haiku-4-5) autonomously passes `runTimeoutSeconds: 60` in `sessions_spawn` calls
- On each successive retry after a timeout, the model **decreases** the value: `60 → 45 → 20 → 30 → 15`
- Each retry is given **less** time than the previous attempt, making success increasingly unlikely
- The subagent was actively making tool calls and producing output — it simply ran out of budget before finishing

## Log Evidence

```
embedded run timeout: runId=... timeoutMs=20000
Error: All models failed (2): model-a: LLM request timed out. | model-b: LLM request timed out.
lane task error: lane=subagent durationMs=60094 error="FailoverError: LLM request timed out."
```

`subagents/runs.json` entries confirm: `runTimeoutSeconds: 60`, `45`, `20`, `30`, `15` across successive spawns.

## Root Cause (from source)

```js
const cfgSubagentTimeout = cfg?.agents?.defaults?.subagents?.runTimeoutSeconds ?? 0;
const runTimeoutSeconds = typeof params.runTimeoutSeconds === "number"
  ? params.runTimeoutSeconds   // model's value wins unconditionally
  : cfgSubagentTimeout;        // config only used if model omits it
```

The model's value is accepted with no floor enforcement. The config cannot constrain what the model passes.

## Requested Feature

A config option to enforce a minimum the model cannot undercut:

- **`agents.defaults.subagents.minRunTimeoutSeconds`** — floor value the model cannot go below
- Or **`agents.defaults.subagents.lockRunTimeoutSeconds`** — config is authoritative, model value ignored

## Workaround

Instructing the orchestrating agent via system prompt to never pass `runTimeoutSeconds` in `sessions_spawn` calls works but is fragile — it relies on the model obeying the instruction every time.

## Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.3.2
- Platform: macOS 15 (Darwin 25.3.0)
- Orchestrator model: claude-haiku-4-5
```

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

- Problem: the Agent Tools dashboard / `tools.effective` inventory originally reported only static OpenClaw tools, so configured bundled MCP tools could be usable at runtime but absent from the session-effective inventory.
- Solution: split the Gateway surface so `tools.effective` is a read-only peek projection with explicit MCP notices, and add `tools.effective.refresh` as an `operator.write` method that performs live MCP discovery/materialization on demand.
- What changed: effective inventory projection supports `source: "mcp"`; session MCP runtimes expose peek-only catalog metadata; the Gateway read path no longer cold-starts MCP transports; the refresh path materializes MCP tools through the existing runtime/materializer/final-policy path; Control UI adds an explicit **Refresh Available Tools** action; SDK/docs/protocol/tests cover the new split.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no provider transport changes, no Tool Search/Code Mode raw provider-body changes, no auto-reply `/tools` command changes, no install-package-dir changes, no broad shared-runtime refactor, and no claim that #80909 or the reporter-specific SSE raw provider-body path is fixed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #: N/A (intentionally not closing #76724; this keeps the reporter-specific SSE/runtime-provider-body path out of scope)
- Related / Refs #76724
- Related #70876
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

#76724 reports MCP handshake success while the Agent Tools dashboard stays stuck at the pre-MCP tool count and does not list MCP tools. Runtime code can materialize bundled MCP tools for model runs, but the dashboard inventory used a separate static inventory path. The initial patch made `tools.effective` perform live MCP materialization; after review of the read/security boundary, this update keeps `tools.effective` read-only and moves live discovery behind an explicit refresh method.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `tools.effective` should not cold-start/list MCP transports from a read-scoped dashboard load, while the dashboard must still have an explicit path to discover configured MCP tools and then show the warm catalog.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw Gateway from this branch at `3c7a4e67b6`, QA mock OpenAI provider, real stdio MCP server created by the proof harness, Tool Search disabled, Code Mode disabled.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_REPO_ROOT=/root/repos/openclaw-mcp-tools-effective \
OPENCLAW_LOCAL_HEAD=3c7a4e67b6 \
OPENCLAW_UPSTREAM_COMMIT=110042d840 \
OPENCLAW_RPC_PROOF_SUMMARY=/tmp/openclaw-mcp-tools-effective-d-proof-summary.json \
node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-mcp-tools-effective-d-proof.ts
```

- Evidence after fix:

Copied live Gateway RPC harness output from this branch:

```json

  "proof": "D read/refresh split: cold read peek-only, explicit refresh materializes MCP, warm read peeks catalog",
  "upstreamCommit": "110042d840",

  "cold": {

    "containsMcpTarget": false,
    "runtimeCreated": false,
    "groupSummaries": [
      { "id": "core", "source": "core", "toolCount": 18 },
      { "id": "plugin", "source": "plugin", "toolCount": 2 }
    ]
  },
  "refreshed": {
    "noticeIds": [],
    "containsMcpTarget": true,
    "matchingIds": ["reproProbe__probe_tool"],
    "runtimeCreated": true,
    "catalogToolNames": ["probe_tool"],
    "groupSummaries": [
      { "id": "core", "source": "core", "toolCount": 18 },
      { "id": "plugin", "source": "plugin", "toolCount": 2 },
      { "id": "mcp", "source": "mcp", "toolCount": 1, "sampleIds": ["reproProbe__probe_tool"] }
    ]
  },
  "warm": {
    "noticeIds": [],
    "containsMcpTarget": true,
    "matchingIds": ["reproProbe__probe_tool"],
    "groupSummaries": [
      { "id": "core", "source": "core", "toolCount": 18 },
      { "id": "plugin", "source": "plugin", "toolCount": 2 },
      { "id": "mcp", "source": "mcp", "toolCount": 1, "sampleIds": ["reproProbe__probe_tool"] }
    ]
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: a cold `tools.effective` read returns only core/plugin groups plus `mcp-not-yet-connected` and does not create a session MCP runtime; explicit `tools.effective.refresh` materializes the configured stdio MCP server and returns `reproProbe__probe_tool`; the next `tools.effective` read shows the warm MCP group via peek without another cold materialization.
- What was not tested: the reporter's original Flask SSE server and release version `v2026.5.2`; Tool Search / Code Mode raw provider-body behavior; #80909; full cross-platform/browser E2E. This PR is scoped to the Gateway/dashboard effective-inventory boundary.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): pre-patch proof on current main returned 20 tools and omitted `reproProbe__probe_tool` from `tools.effective` while the runtime provider path could include it.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `tools.effective` called the static `resolveEffectiveToolInventory()` resolver, which builds from `createOpenClawCodingTools()` only. Bundled MCP tools are appended later in the model-run path through the session MCP runtime/materializer, so the dashboard inventory and runtime provider path diverged.
- Boundary correction in this update: live MCP discovery/listing can start configured stdio/remote transports, so it now lives behind explicit `operator.write` `tools.effective.refresh`; the read-scoped method only peeks at an existing matching-fingerprint runtime catalog and otherwise returns a notice.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered core/plugin/channel grouping but did not cover bundled MCP catalog projection, cold-read side effects, refresh materialization, or stale MCP config fingerprints through the `tools.effective` handler/RPC path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end-style Gateway RPC proof
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/tools-effective.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools-effective-inventory.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`
  - `packages/sdk/src/index.test.ts`
  - `packages/sdk/src/index.e2e.test.ts`
  - `ui/src/ui/controllers/agents.test.ts`
  - `ui/src/ui/views/agents-panels-tools-skills.browser.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: cold `tools.effective` does not create/list MCP and returns notices; warm read projects an existing same-fingerprint catalog; fingerprint mismatch returns refresh-needed notice instead of stale MCP tools; explicit `tools.effective.refresh` materializes MCP tools and applies final effective policy; UI uses read on normal load and refresh on explicit action.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug and security-boundary concern live at the seam between static inventory, session MCP runtime, Gateway method scope, SDK, and UI refresh behavior.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none found for bundled MCP in `tools-effective` / `tools-effective-inventory` before this patch.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The Agent Tools panel now loads without implicitly starting configured MCP transports. If MCP servers are configured but not connected/listed yet, the panel shows an explanatory notice and a **Refresh Available Tools** action. Triggering refresh performs live discovery and lets subsequent reads show the warm MCP tools.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before this PR:
[tools.effective] -> [static createOpenClawCodingTools inventory] -> [core/plugin/channel only]

Updated design:
[tools.effective]         -> [static inventory + peek existing MCP catalog] -> [core/plugin/channel + notices or warm mcp]
[tools.effective.refresh] -> [session MCP runtime materialize] -> [final bundled-tool policy] -> [core/plugin/channel + mcp]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No new capability beyond existing configured MCP materialization; yes, a new explicit Gateway method is added.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? Yes, only from explicit `tools.effective.refresh` or existing run-lifecycle MCP materialization, not from cold `tools.effective` reads.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes, refresh can start/list configured MCP transports; it does not invoke MCP tools.
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: live MCP li

github_context:

- Type: github_pr

- Title: fix(browser): honor image sanitization config for screenshots
- State: CLOSED
- Author: xx205
- Labels: size: S, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient, P2, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, status: 👀 ready for maintainer look

Body:
```markdown
## Summary
- forward `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` into browser screenshot tool results
- forward the same image sanitization limits into `snapshot --labels` image results
- add regression coverage for both browser image-returning paths

## Problem
`browser screenshot` normalized images to the browser layer's `<= 2000px` limit first, but then returned them through `imageResultFromFile()` without passing runtime image sanitization config.

That meant the final tool image still fell back to the default `1200px` sanitize limit, even when `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` was configured higher. The same omission existed for `browser snapshot --labels`.

## Fix
Thread runtime image sanitization limits into both browser image-returning call sites:
- `browser:screenshot`
- `browser:snapshot` when `labels=true`

This keeps browser tool image behavior aligned with the documented global image sanitization setting and with other OpenClaw image paths that already forward this config.

## Verification
- focused browser extension test suite passed:
  - `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts` (`51 passed`)
- direct runtime check with the same input image showed:
  - default config: final browser screenshot output still reduced to `1200x625`
  - `imageMaxDimensionPx = 1920`: final output became `1920x1000`
  - `imageMaxDimensionPx = 2000`: final output stayed at `2000x1042`
  - `imageMaxDimensionPx = 3000`: final output still stayed at `2000x1042`, as expected because browser pre-normalization already caps screenshots at `2000px`

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed**: browser screenshot and `browser snapshot --labels` image results ignored `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` on the final tool-image sanitize step, so a browser screenshot could still come out at `1200px` even when the config was set higher.

**Real environment tested**: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS using the real browser screenshot normalization path plus the browser tool image-result path with a local PNG test image as the input.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran the browser screenshot runtime check against the patched branch, varying `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` across default, `1920`, `2000`, and `3000`, then inspected the generated image metadata.

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
live output from runtime image metadata inspection:
normalized browser layer: 2000x1042, 246537 bytes, image/jpeg
default final output:    1200x625,  107491 bytes, image/jpeg
configured 1920:         1920x1000, 214162 bytes, image/jpeg
configured 2000:         2000x1042, 246537 bytes, image/jpeg
configured 3000:         2000x1042, 246537 bytes, image/jpeg
```

**Observed result after fix**: after the patch, browser-originated image results now honor the configured sanitize limit up to the browser layer's own `2000px` ceiling. `1920` produces `1920x1000`, `2000` produces `2000x1042`, and `3000` does not exceed `2000x1042` because browser pre-normalization already caps that path.

**What was not tested**: a full remote provider call was not run; this proof covers the local browser screenshot normalization path and the actual browser tool image packaging and sanitize path.

```
