Greptile Summary

This PR correctly patches an SSRF vulnerability in `minimaxUnderstandImage()` by replacing the raw `fetch()` call with `fetchWithSsrFGuard(withStrictGuardedFetchMode(...))`, which resolves and validates the target hostname against the SSRF blocklist (private IPs, cloud metadata endpoints, etc.) and pins DNS to prevent rebinding attacks before any request is sent. A `finally` block is also added to ensure the dispatcher is released after the request completes. **Key observations:** - The core security fix is correct: `GUARDED_FETCH_MODE.STRICT` is the right mode for an external third-party API — it does not trust environment proxy settings and pins the resolved IP. - `auditContext` is not set on the `fetchWithSsrFGuard` call, so SSRF-blocked log lines will emit the generic `"url-fetch"` label instead of something identifiable like `"minimax-vlm"`. This makes security monitoring harder without affecting correctness. - The `try { ... } finally { release() }` block is placed *after* the `await fetchWithSsrFGuard(...)` call rather than wrapping it. This is functionally safe (the guarded fetch handles its own cleanup on error), but the asymmetry is subtle and could confuse future maintainers.

github_context:

- Type: github_pr

- Title: fix(session): prefer real tool result over synthetic error in transcript repair
- State: OPEN
- Author: Jerry-Xin
- Labels: agents, size: S, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, proof: sufficient, P1, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, merge-risk: 🚨 automation, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, status: 👀 ready for maintainer look

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Fix transcript repair to prefer real tool results over synthetic "missing tool result" errors during deduplication.

When a channel (e.g. Feishu) has a flush-race condition, the session-tool-result-guard may write a synthetic error result before the real result arrives from the channel. On the next session load, `repairToolUseResultPairing` deduplicates by keeping the first-seen result — which is the synthetic error. The user sees "Something went wrong" even though the tool call actually succeeded.

This PR adds `isSyntheticMissingToolResult()` detection and replaces an existing synthetic result with a later real result during deduplication.

> **Note:** This is a transcript-repair mitigation. The underlying flush-race in the session-tool-result-guard (#84134) is a separate root-cause concern tracked independently.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `session-transcript-repair.ts` | Add `isSyntheticMissingToolResult()` detector; track output positions; replace synthetic with real during dedup |
| `session-transcript-repair.test.ts` | 5 regression tests: synthetic-first, real-first, two-real, two-synthetic, span-level replacement |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/session-transcript-repair.test.ts
 ✓ unit-fast ../../src/agents/session-transcript-repair.test.ts (40 tests) 5ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  40 passed (40)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When a synthetic "missing tool result" error is persisted before the real result arrives (Feishu channel flush race), the repair pass keeps the synthetic error and the user sees "Something went wrong" even though the tool call succeeded.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, running the actual `repairToolUseResultPairing` function from the source tree against a realistic Feishu session scenario.

### Failing proof before fix (main branch)

```
$ npx tsx proof-84419-runner.mts  # on main branch

--- Input: session with synthetic-first, real-second tool result ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2 (1 synthetic error, 1 real success)
  Scenario: Feishu flush race — synthetic error persisted before real result arrived

--- Output: after repair ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2
  Kept result isError: true
  Kept result text: "[openclaw] missing tool result in session history; ..."

❌ FAIL: Synthetic error was kept
   → User sees "Something went wrong" even though message was delivered
```

### Passing proof after fix (this PR)

```
$ npx tsx proof-84419-runner.mts  # on fix branch

--- Input: session with synthetic-first, real-second tool result ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2 (1 synthetic error, 1 real success)

--- Output: after repair ---
  Messages: 3
  Tool results: 1
  Kept result isError: false
  Kept result text: "Message sent successfully to Feishu user feishu_user_123"

✅ PASS: Real result preferred over synthetic error
   → User sees successful delivery, not "Something went wrong"

--- Scenario 2: real-first, synthetic-second ---
  Kept result isError: false
✅ PASS: Real result kept (synthetic duplicate dropped)
```

- **Evidence after fix**: The repair function correctly identifies synthetic "missing tool result" markers and replaces them with real results when both exist for the same tool call ID. Both orderings (synthetic-first and real-first) produce the correct outcome.
- **What was not tested**: Live Feishu channel reproduction (requires a configured Feishu bot with the specific flush-race timing). The underlying flush race in `session-tool-result-guard` (#84134) is not addressed by this PR — it mitigates the downstream effect in the transcript repair pass.

```

github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 65364 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/65364 - Title: feat(plugins): add registerProviderRuntimeAuthOverride API - State: OPEN - Author: supersantux - Labels: gateway, agents, size: L, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 auth-provider, merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary Add a public plugin registration API, `registerProviderRuntimeAuthOverride`, that lets external plugins provide runtime auth for specific built-in model providers without replacing the provider implementation. This is intended for integrations that need brokered, short-lived, vault-backed, or proxy-routed credentials for built-in providers like `anthropic`, `openai`, or `openrouter`, and currently end up carrying downstream patches in `src/agents/model-auth.ts`. ## What Changed - Added `api.registerProviderRuntimeAuthOverride({ providers, run })` to the plugin SDK. - Added runtime resolution support in `src/agents/model-auth.ts`. - Added a dedicated global module for active override registrations: `src/plugins/provider-runtime-auth-override-global.ts`. This keeps override state in sync with the active registry without widening the hook-runner global state. - Added a reentrancy guard so a plugin override can safely call back into `resolveApiKeyForProvider()` via the public SDK helper without infinite recursion. - Added plugin-id error logging when an override throws. - Tightened `ProviderRuntimeAuthOverrideResult.mode` to the auth-mode union and kept runtime validation as defense-in-depth. - Added an opt-in registration-based availability signal for callers that only need a coarse "may be available" answer, while keeping default availability checks conservative. ## API Shape ```ts export type ProviderRuntimeAuthOverrideContext = { provider: string; modelId: string; profileId?: string; }; export type ProviderRuntimeAuthOverrideResult = { apiKey: string; mode?: "api-key" | "oauth" | "token" | "aws-sdk"; source?: string; baseUrl?: string; providerRequestHeaders?: Record; }; export type ProviderRuntimeAuthOverride = { providers: string[]; run: ( ctx: ProviderRuntimeAuthOverrideContext, ) => Promise; }; ``` Example: ```ts api.registerProviderRuntimeAuthOverride({ providers: ["anthropic", "openai", "openrouter"], run: async (ctx) => { const token = await resolveBrokeredToken(ctx.provider); return token ? { apiKey: token, source: "broker:runtime", providerRequestHeaders: { "x-session": sessionId() }, } : null; }, }); ``` ## Semantics 1. OpenClaw resolves provider and model normally. 2. Registered overrides for that provider are evaluated in registration order. 3. The first non-null result wins. 4. `null` / `undefined` means "no override, continue default behavior." 5. Throw means fail the request after logging the plugin id; there is no implicit fallback. 6. Unknown provider ids in the registration list are ignored. 7. If an override calls `resolveApiKeyForProvider()` internally, the inner call skips overrides and falls through to normal resolution. ## Scope Notes - `baseUrl` and `providerRequestHeaders` are applied in the main LLM inference runner path. - Side paths such as media understanding, image generation, and TTS currently consume only the override `apiKey` in v1. - `hasAvailableAuthForProvider()` stays conservative by default because overrides may legally return `null` at runtime; callers that only need a coarse UI signal can opt into treating registration as "may be available." - `resolveModelAuthMode()` is intentionally unchanged in this PR. A meaningful override-aware mode result would need static mode metadata on the registration API rather than a heuristic. ## Why A Dedicated Global Module This PR originally stored active override registrations alongside the hook-runner global state. During review and implementation, that proved too easy to conflate with unrelated hook-runner lifecycle concerns. The final shape keeps the feature but moves override registration state into a dedicated singleton module: - clearer ownership - smaller surface area - easier reasoning for tests and registry swaps - no widening of `GlobalHookRunnerRegistry` ## Security Impact - New capability: a trusted plugin can opt in to runtime auth overrides for listed providers. - The override callback receives only `{ provider, modelId, profileId? }`. - The API does not expose already-resolved secrets from other providers. - Throwing is explicit failure, not silent fallback. - Override activity and thrown plugin ids are logged. ## Verification Focused regression coverage was added for: - override applied - provider mismatch - null result fallthrough - thrown override - first non-null wins - unknown mode normalization - `baseUrl` and `providerRequestHeaders` passthrough - reentrancy guard - active-registry swap synchronization - runtime reset synchronization - `hasAvailableAuthForProvider()` with registered overrides - dedicated global override singleton behavior Local command run: ```bash npx vitest run src/agents/model-auth.test.ts src/plugins/runtime.test.ts src/plugins/runtime.channel-pin.test.ts src/plugins/hook-runner-global.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime-auth-override-global.test.ts ``` Result: - 5 test files passed - 76 tests passed ## Compatibility - Fully additive. If no plugin registers an override, behavior is unchanged. - No config migration required. ## Optional Follow-ups 1. Extend `baseUrl` / header override application consistently across side paths such as media understanding, image generation, and TTS. 2. Add plugin SDK docs with a concrete override example. 3. Consider explicit static metadata if `resolveModelAuthMode()` ever needs to reflect runtime overrides accurately. ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-04-12T13:39:46Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR adds a `registerProviderRuntimeAuthOverride` plugin API that lets external plugins supply runtime credentials for built-in model providers, with first-non-null-wins semantics and an explicit throw-to-fail contract. The overall design is clean and additive — no existing behavior changes when no plugin registers an override. - **P1:** The override invocation in `resolveApiKeyForProvider` (lines 372–376) has no try/catch. The PR's own Security Impact section calls out \"the failing plugin id is logged\" as a mitigation, but the logging is absent; operators cannot diagnose which plugin caused an auth failure. - **P2:** `result.mode` (typed `string`) is blindly cast to the `ResolvedProviderAuth` mode union without validation; an invalid string from a plugin silently passes through to downstream auth-mode branching. - **P2:** `hasAvailableAuthForProvider` is not updated to check overrides, so auth-availability indicators will show a provider as unauthenticated even when an override would succeed.

Confidence Score: 4/5

Safe to merge after fixing the missing try/catch + plugin-id logging on override throw. One P1 finding: the missing error logging directly contradicts the documented security mitigation and leaves operators without an audit trail when an override throws. The two P2 findings (mode cast, hasAvailableAuthForProvider) are quality issues that don't break current callers but should be addressed before GA promotion of this API. src/agents/model-auth.ts — override invocation block (lines 361–391) and hasAvailableAuthForProvider (lines 632–681).

Security Review

 - **Missing plugin-id logging on override throw** (`src/agents/model-auth.ts` lines 372–376): The PR's security mitigation states \"the failing plugin id is logged\" when an override throws, but there is no try/catch and no log statement around `reg.override.run()`. Operators cannot audit which plugin caused an auth failure. - **Unvalidated `mode` field from plugin result** (`src/agents/model-auth.ts` line 384): `result.mode` (typed `string`) is cast directly to `\"api-key\" | \"oauth\" | \"token\" | \"aws-sdk\"` without checking the value. A plugin returning an unrecognized mode string could silently alter downstream auth-mode branching.

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 44049 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/44049 - Title: [AI-assisted] Fix duplicated acp server args in ACP client - State: OPEN - Author: t0yohei - Labels: size: XS, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, proof: sufficient, P2, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, status: 👀 ready for maintainer look Body: ```markdown ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: `openclaw acp client` always prefixes the spawned server command with `acp`, so if an upstream caller also passes `serverArgs=["acp", ...]`, the effective command becomes `openclaw acp acp ...` and fails. - Why it matters: this makes ACP client startup brittle and causes session creation to fail for callers that already include the subcommand in forwarded args. - What changed: normalize `serverArgs` in the ACP client by dropping one leading `acp` when present, then prepend the canonical `acp` subcommand exactly once; added a regression test for this case. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no changes to ACP server behavior, permissions, routing, session semantics, or any non-ACP CLI paths. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # ## User-visible / Behavior Changes ACP client startup is now tolerant of callers that redundantly include `acp` in forwarded `serverArgs`. Before: `openclaw acp client --server openclaw --server-args acp --server-verbose` could spawn `openclaw acp acp --verbose` and fail. After: the same invocation normalizes to `openclaw acp --verbose` and proceeds to session creation. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`) - Data access scope changed? (`No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: This changes ACP client command construction only by deduping a redundant leading `acp` token in forwarded server args. Risk is low because the change narrows an invalid invocation into the canonical one instead of expanding execution surface. Mitigation: added a regression test covering the duplicated-`acp` case. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local host runtime - Model/provider: openai-codex/gpt-5.4 - Integration/channel (if any): ACP CLI - Relevant config (redacted): default local OpenClaw CLI setup; no special ACP config required for repro ### Steps 1. Run `openclaw acp client --server openclaw --server-args acp --server-verbose` 2. Observe the spawned server command 3. Attempt session creation ### Expected - The ACP client should spawn the canonical server command once as `openclaw acp --verbose` - Session creation should proceed successfully ### Actual - Before the fix, this could spawn `openclaw acp acp --verbose` - That failed with `error: too many arguments for 'acp'` ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Before: ```text [acp-client] spawning: openclaw acp acp --verbose error: too many arguments for 'acp' ``` After: ```text [acp-client] spawning: openclaw acp --verbose [acp-client] creating session ``` Targeted test: ```text npx vitest run src/acp/client.test.ts ``` ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Reproduced the failing duplicated-acp invocation locally - Verified the same invocation now spawns openclaw acp --verbose - Verified it reaches session creation successfully - Ran npx vitest run src/acp/client.test.ts - Edge cases checked: - callers that already pass leading acp in serverArgs - existing --server-verbose behavior remains intact after normalization - What you did **not** verify: - full repository green on pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test - broader ACP server behavior outside this argument-normalization path ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (Yes) - Config/env changes? (No) - Migration needed? (No) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR / commit - Files/config to restore: src/acp/client.ts, src/acp/client.test.ts - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: - ACP client spawning malformed commands - regression where valid serverArgs are incorrectly altered ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: overly broad normalization could accidentally rewrite intended server args - Mitigation: normalization only removes a single leading acp; all other args are preserved verbatim - Risk: reviewers may assume the full repository test/build matrix is green - Mitigation: this PR explicitly reports targeted verification only; local pnpm build and pnpm test currently fail due to unrelated existing repository issues outside this change ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-12T12:58:17Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes a bug in `buildServerArgs` where a caller already including `"acp"` in forwarded `serverArgs` would cause the spawned command to receive the subcommand twice (e.g. `openclaw acp acp --verbose`). The one-line normalization in `client.ts` is correct and appropriately narrow. The main concern is the regression test: the newly added `"dedupes a leading acp server arg from callers"` test is byte-for-byte identical to the pre-existing `"keeps non-windows invocation unchanged"` test and does **not** cover the fix. Both tests call `resolveAcpClientSpawnInvocation`, which forwards `serverArgs` directly to `materializeWindowsSpawnProgram` without going through `buildServerArgs`. Reverting the fix leaves both tests green, so no real regression guard exists for this change. Key points: - `client.ts` fix is logically sound and properly scoped. - `client.test.ts` regression test is a duplicate of an existing test and provides no coverage of the actual `buildServerArgs` change. - To truly guard against regression, `buildServerArgs` should be exported and unit-tested, or `createAcpClient` should be tested end-to-end with a mocked spawn.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- The production fix is correct and low-risk, but the claimed regression test does not exercise the fix, leaving the behavior unguarded. - The change to `buildServerArgs` is narrowly scoped and logically correct. Score is reduced because the regression test is a no-op duplicate — it calls `resolveAcpClientSpawnInvocation` (which bypasses `buildServerArgs`), so the fix could be silently reverted without any test failing. The PR description explicitly states the full test/build matrix is not green, which adds minor additional uncertainty. - `src/acp/client.test.ts` needs attention — the new test must be rewritten to actually invoke `buildServerArgs` or `createAcpClient` to provide meaningful regression coverage.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/acp/client.test.ts Line: 155-170 Comment: **Regression test does not exercise the fix** The new test is byte-for-byte identical to the existing `"keeps non-windows invocation unchanged"` test (same input, same expected output, same platform) and does **not** cover the `buildServerArgs` deduplication introduced in `client.ts`. The reason is that `resolveAcpClientSpawnInvocation` (what both tests call) receives `serverArgs` as a plain parameter and forwards it directly to `materializeWindowsSpawnProgram` — it never calls `buildServerArgs`. The deduplication logic lives in `buildServerArgs`, which is only exercised by `createAcpClient`. Concretely, if you revert the two-line change in `buildServerArgs`, this test still passes (as does the original test). The fix is therefore untested. To actually cover the regression, the test should either: 1. Export `buildServerArgs` (or an internal helper) and unit-test it, e.g.: ```ts // src/acp/client.ts — export for testing export function buildServerArgs(opts: AcpClientOptions): string[] { ... } ``` ```ts it("dedupes a leading acp server arg from callers", () => { expect(buildServerArgs({ serverArgs: ["acp", "--verbose"] })) .toEqual(["acp", "--verbose"]); // without leading acp is unchanged expect(buildServerArgs({ serverArgs: ["--verbose"] })) .toEqual(["acp", "--verbose"]); }); ``` 2. Or mock `spawn`/`resolveAcpClientSpawnInvocation` and call `createAcpClient` end-to-end, asserting on the args that reach the spawn call. As-is, the regression can silently re-appear without any test failing. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

The root fix was already landed in #68574 (2026.4.18). This PR tightens the normalization logic and adds coverage to prevent regressions. ## Test plan - [x] `pnpm test extensions/openrouter` passes (8 tests) - [x] `pnpm test extensions/arcee` passes (7 tests) - [x] `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.test.ts` passes (50 tests) Fixes #69646 ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-04-21T11:45:47Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR adds `.toLowerCase()` to the `normalizeBaseUrl` helper in three files (`extensions/openrouter/provider-catalog.ts`, `extensions/arcee/provider-catalog.ts`, `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.provider-runtime.test-support.ts`) to make OpenRouter URL detection case-insensitive, and adds regression tests for uppercase variants, already-correct URLs, and custom proxy passthrough. The changes are minimal and correct: the lowercased intermediate value is only used for comparison against known-lowercase constants, so the actual URL returned is always the canonical `OPENROUTER_BASE_URL` constant — not the case-folded input.

Safe to merge — small, well-tested fix with no side effects outside of URL comparison. All three changed helpers use `.toLowerCase()` solely to compare against pre-lowercased constants; the value returned to callers is always the hardcoded `OPENROUTER_BASE_URL` constant, never the case-folded input. New tests cover the uppercase variant, the already-correct no-op path, and the custom-proxy passthrough. No P0/P1 findings. No files require special attention. target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #84816: perf(models): /models 20s → 5ms via pre-warmed provider auth state
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_pr
- Number: 84816
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/84816
- Title: perf(models): /models 20s → 5ms via pre-warmed provider auth state
- State: OPEN
- Author: sjf
- Labels: gateway, agents, maintainer, size: S

Body:
```markdown
Listing models went from **~55 s cold / ~20 s every subsequent call** to **~5 ms (pre-warmed)** — a ~4,100× speedup on the hot path. Every `/models` call (Discord/Telegram pickers, CLI, status commands) now short-circuits through a prepared provider-auth map built once at gateway startup instead of rediscovering every provider's auth state from scratch on every invocation.

This also fixes the "This interaction failed" error in Discord's `/models` picker. Discord requires bots to ack or defer an interaction within 3 seconds. With the gateway's event loop blocked on a ~20 s auth-discovery sweep, the immediate `interaction.acknowledge()` in the picker handler couldn't actually run inside Discord's 3 s window — Discord invalidated the interaction token and the picker rendered the failure state. Cutting the hot-path work to ~5 ms keeps the gateway responsive so the ack happens immediately.

## Summary

`buildModelsProviderData` was spending ~20 s in `resolveVisibleModelCatalog`'s auth-filter loop — 30+ unique providers × ~600 ms each of plugin-runtime / external-CLI / auth-profile-store discovery, redone fresh on every call. The catalog itself was already cached via `modelCatalogPromise`; the per-provider auth check was the duplicate-lookup-branch the user-facing pickers were eating on every interaction.

This moves the canonical fact earlier: compute a `Map<provider, boolean>` once at gateway startup via `warmCurrentProviderAuthState`, hold it module-level, and have `hasAuthForModelProvider` consult it first. If a provider's answer is in the prepared map the function returns immediately; otherwise it falls through to the existing compute path. Invalidated on config reload alongside `resetModelCatalogCache` so the next read after a relevant config change rewarms.

Per CLAUDE.md "move the canonical fact earlier; reuse prepared runtime objects; delete duplicate lookup branches" — no new caches scattered through request-time code, no per-channel plumbing, no new SDK surface. One module-level prepared object with an explicit set / clear lifecycle modeled on `PluginMetadataSnapshot`.

## Verification

Benchmarked on local dev gateway (`./run.sh openclaw gateway run`), catalog of 955 entries / 40 unique providers / 167 visible after auth, single machine same config, captured back-to-back. Trigger was `/models` via Discord (`@sjf_claw`) — same code path every other model-listing call hits.

Behavior addressed: `buildModelsProviderData` (the chokepoint behind `/models` / model pickers) recomputes the per-provider auth state on every call.
Real environment tested: macOS local openclaw dev, catalog with bundled + external plugins, Discord channel live.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: SIGINT the gateway to rebuild on this branch; let warmer complete after `[gateway] gateway ready`; trigger `/models` 12 times in Discord; grep `sjfsjfsjf buildModelsProviderData done` from `/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-*.log` (instrumentation since stripped before commit).
Evidence after fix: see Timings table below. Raw samples preserved in `PICKER-PERF-TIMINGS.md` in the dev workspace.
Observed result after fix: per-call `buildModelsProviderData` dropped from ~20.5 s (n=8) to ~5 ms (n=12). Discord no longer shows "This interaction failed" on the picker.
What was not tested: Telegram + CLI `/models` paths (same code path, expected to benefit identically); Crabbox; multi-agent gateways where multiple `agentId` values flow through the picker (the prepared map is cfg-keyed, not agent-keyed — both before this PR and after, `hasAuthForModelProvider` is agent-agnostic).

### Timings

BEFORE (HEAD without this patch), n=8 subsequent + 1 cold:

| Metric | ms |
|---|---|
| First call (cold catalog) | 54,812 |
| Subsequent calls min | 20,403 |
| Subsequent calls median | ~20,570 |
| Subsequent calls max | 20,766 |
| Subsequent calls mean | ~20,569 |

AFTER (this PR), n=12 post-warm + warmer:

| Metric | ms |
|---|---|
| Hot-path call min | 1 |
| Hot-path call median | ~5 |
| Hot-path call max | 6 |
| Hot-path call mean | ~4.3 |
| One-time gateway-startup warm (40 providers) | 49,203 |

Headline:

| Scenario | BEFORE | AFTER | Speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| First `/models` after restart | 54,812 ms | ~5 ms (post-warm) | ~10,000× |
| Every subsequent `/models` | ~20,569 ms | ~5 ms | ~4,100× |
| One-time startup warm cost | n/a | 49,203 ms | — |

The 49 s warm cost is not a regression: it's the same auth-discovery sweep the BEFORE workload was paying on every call, plus the cold-catalog `loadModelCatalog` (~26 s) which BEFORE also pays on its first call. AFTER pays both once at startup. On a reload that clears only the auth state (catalog already cached), warm is the ~23 s auth portion only.

```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #72021: Short-term promotion: signalCount mixes daily/session signals with real recalls
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 72021
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/72021
- Title: Short-term promotion: signalCount mixes daily/session signals with real recalls
- State: OPEN
- Author: CSZ-w
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, clawsweeper:linked-pr-open, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Bug description

Short-term memory promotion (`rankShortTermPromotionCandidates`) uses `totalSignalCountForEntry` to gate minRecallCount, but that function sums recallCount + dailyCount + groundedCount. Daily and session signals (from ingestDailyMemorySignals / ingestSessionTranscriptSignals) are counted equally with real user search recalls. This produces:

1. **False candidates promoted:** entries with recallCount=0 but dailyCount=3 pass the minRecallCount=3 filter
2. **Real candidates hidden:** entries with recallCount=3 score 0.64-0.68, below the minScore=0.80 threshold, because the scoring weights were tuned to work with the inflated signal counts

## Root cause chain

### 1. Signal type mixing

```ts
function totalSignalCountForEntry(entry) {
  return (entry.recallCount ?? 0) + (entry.dailyCount ?? 0) + (entry.groundedCount ?? 0);
}
```

The dailyCount comes from ingestDailyMemorySignals which scans past 30 days of memory files and stamps every matching entry as a daily signal. These are NOT user search recalls but are counted the same way.

### 2. Score inflation from combined signals

avgScore = totalScore / signalCount. When signalCount is inflated by daily signals, avgScore rises. An entry with recallCount=3 and dailyCount=3 has signalCount=6, giving avgScore higher than a real recall-only entry with signalCount=3.

### 3. Real entries fail minScore

Verified data from short-term-recall.json:

```json
{
  "key": "memory:memory/2026-04-16.md:407:425",
  "recallCount": 3,
  "dailyCount": 0,
  "queryHashes": 3,
  "recallDays": 4,
  "totalScore": 1.367,
  "computedPromotionScore": 0.645
}
```

With minScore=0.80, this entry is filtered out. The entry IS actively recalled by user search, but unreachable with current scoring.

## Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.4.24
- short-term-recall.json: 10,429 entries, 8 with recallCount >= 3, 2 with recallCount >= 3 AND queries >= 3 AND not promoted
- minRecallCount=3, minScore=0.80, minUniqueQueries=3

## Existing workaround

Three patches applied to compiled JS:
1. Light dreaming sort: prioritize recallCount over lastRecalledAt
2. Rehydration span: widen from +3/+8 to +15/+20
3. Make totalSignalCountForEntry only count recallCount

After these, the system honestly reports applied=0 - no noise promoted, but legitimate entries still cannot pass minScore.

## Suggestion

recordShortTermRecalls should weight recall signals differently from daily/session signals when aggregating totalScore. Or alternatively: separate the minRecallCount gate to use only recallCount (not combined signals) while keeping the combined signalCount for totalScore calculation.

```

github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 44086 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/44086 - Title: fix(agents): assistant message content null instead of empty string breaks OpenAI-compatible providers - State: OPEN - Author: xinhuagu - Labels: agents, size: S, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Problem When an OpenAI-compatible provider (e.g. DeepSeek v3.1) returns an assistant message with `content: ""` and `tool_calls`, the empty text block is filtered out during message conversion in `convertMessages`, causing the content to default to `null`. Providers that require string content then reject the request with: ``` Messages token length must be in (0, 1048576], but got 0 ``` This happens because `convertMessages` initializes assistant content as `null` (for providers without `requiresAssistantAfterToolResult`), then filters text blocks with `b.text && b.text.trim().length > 0`, which removes empty strings. The assistant message is kept (it has `tool_calls`), but with `content: null`. ## Solution Add an `onPayload` wrapper (`createAssistantNullContentFixWrapper`) that normalizes `content: null` to `""` on assistant messages carrying `tool_calls` for `openai-completions` API payloads. This is safe because: - OpenAI and most providers accept both `null` and `""` for assistant content - Providers like DeepSeek require string content, not null - The wrapper only activates for `openai-completions` API (not Responses API) - Existing string content is never modified ## Test plan 4 test cases covering: - Null content normalized to empty string when tool_calls present - Null content preserved when no tool_calls (no unnecessary modification) - Non-openai-completions APIs unaffected - Existing string content preserved Fixes #43716 ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-12T13:56:12Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes a compatibility issue with OpenAI-compatible providers (e.g. DeepSeek) that reject `content: null` on assistant messages that carry `tool_calls`. The root cause is that `convertMessages` in `pi-ai` filters out empty text blocks and leaves `content` as `null` when no text content survives, which some stricter providers reject with a token-length error. The fix introduces a lightweight `onPayload` wrapper (`createAssistantNullContentFixWrapper`) that normalizes `null` → `""` for these messages at the last moment before the HTTP request is made, scoped exclusively to `openai-completions` payloads. The approach is consistent with the established wrapper pattern already used for service-tier injection and Responses API context management. Key points: - The wrapper only activates for `api === "openai-completions"`, leaving Responses API payloads and other providers untouched. - Only assistant messages that simultaneously have `content == null` **and** non-empty `tool_calls` are modified; plain assistant messages without tool calls are left as-is. - Existing non-null string content is never overwritten. - Four unit tests cover all relevant cases (normalization, no-op when no tool_calls, API-type gating, and preservation of existing content). - The wrapper is applied after the existing `createOpenAIResponsesContextManagementWrapper`, but since the two wrappers gate on mutually exclusive API types there is no ordering concern.

Confidence Score: 5/5

- This PR is safe to merge — it is a narrow, well-tested fix that only mutates payloads for a specific API type and condition. - The change is minimal and surgical: a single new wrapper function gated on `api === "openai-completions"` that only touches assistant messages with `tool_calls` and `null` content. target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #6615: Feature: Add denylist support for exec-approvals
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 6615
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/6615
- Title: Feature: Add denylist support for exec-approvals
- State: OPEN
- Author: aaroneden
- Labels: enhancement, P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:needs-security-review, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:data-loss, impact:security, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Add **denylist** support to exec-approvals, complementing the existing allowlist. This enables "allow everything except X" policies.

## Use Case

I want to:
- Allow all commands to run without prompts
- **Except** block/prompt for specific dangerous commands like:
  - `gog gmail send` (sending emails)
  - `gog gmail delete` (deleting emails)  
  - `beeper-send.sh` (sending messages)

Currently, exec-approvals only supports:
- `security: "deny"` - block all
- `security: "allowlist"` - allow only listed commands
- `security: "full"` - allow everything

There's no way to say "allow everything except these specific patterns."

## Proposed Solution

Add a `denylist` array to exec-approvals config:

```json
{
  "defaults": {
    "security": "denylist",
    "ask": "on-match",
    "denylist": [
      {
        "id": "DENY-EMAIL-SEND",
        "pattern": "/opt/homebrew/bin/gog",
        "argsMatch": "gmail send",
        "reason": "Sending emails requires approval"
      },
      {
        "id": "DENY-EMAIL-DELETE",
        "pattern": "/opt/homebrew/bin/gog", 
        "argsMatch": "gmail delete",
        "reason": "Use 'gmail archive' instead"
      },
      {
        "id": "DENY-BEEPER-SEND",
        "pattern": "*/beeper-send.sh",
        "reason": "Sending messages requires approval"
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

### Key additions:
- `security: "denylist"` mode - allow everything except matches
- `argsMatch` field - match against command arguments, not just binary path
- `ask: "on-match"` - prompt when denylist matches (vs blocking outright)

## Current Workarounds

1. Use `security: "full"` and hope agent guardrails work (unreliable)
2. Use `security: "allowlist"` with a massive list of safe commands (tedious)
3. Create wrapper scripts (fragile)

## Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.1.30
- macOS
```

Comments/context:
```markdown
- SmallNew2003 at 2026-02-09T10:41:57Z:
+1 for this feature!

My use case is slightly different but the same need:
- Allow all common commands to run freely
- Block only extremely dangerous disk operations: `dd`, `mkfs.*`, `fdisk`, `parted`, `wipefs`, etc.

Currently using a massive allowlist (300+ commands) as a workaround, but it's tedious to maintain and easy to miss edge cases.

A denylist mode would be much more practical for "allow everything except these few dangerous patterns" scenarios.

- AUTHENSOR at 2026-02-13T19:35:42Z:
This is a common pain point. The allow-everything-except pattern is what most developers actually want, because maintaining an exhaustive allowlist is impractical and a full deny breaks the workflow.

Your proposed denylist approach is solid. A few thoughts from building a similar system:

**Pattern matching on arguments, not just binary paths, is the right call.** The same binary (`gog`) can be safe or dangerous depending on arguments. Matching on the full command string with regex gives you the granularity you need without maintaining separate entries for every subcommand.

**The tri-state model (allow / deny / require-approval) covers more ground than just deny.** Your `ask: "on-match"` is essentially "require-approval," which is the right default for most denylist matches. Hard-deny should be reserved for things that are never acceptable (like `rm -rf /` or posting credentials). Everything else benefits from a human confirmation step rather than a hard block, because sometimes the agent has a legitimate reason.

**Deny-by-default is safer than denylist-by-default.** With a denylist approach, anything you forgot to add is allowed. With deny-by-default, anything you forgot to add is blocked. The tradeoff is more upfront config work, but it means a novel attack vector does not automatically get through. Worth considering as an option alongside the denylist mode.

**Credential file patterns deserve special handling.** Regardless of the security mode, access to `.env`, `.aws/credentials`, `*.pem`, and similar files should probably be blocked by default. These are the most common real-world incidents with AI agents, and they are almost never something you want to allow.

For reference, [SafeClaw](https://github.com/AUTHENSOR/SafeClaw) implements this as a standalone policy engine with YAML-defined rules. Policies support allow/deny/require-approval per action type with regex pattern matching on arguments. It evaluates in sub-millisecond and logs every decision to an audit trail. The deny-by-default model means the config starts safe and you explicitly open up what the agent needs.

The `argsMatch` field in your proposal is the right abstraction. One suggestion: support full regex rather than just substring matching. Patterns like `gmail (send|delete|trash)` are more maintainable than separate entries for each subcommand.

- ai-cre at 2026-03-16T00:58:31Z:
CRE has this. always_block patterns in rules.json with regex matching. Plus always_allow and needs_llm_review layers. Works as OpenClaw plugin. https://github.com/tech-and-ai/claude-rule-enforcer

- Artyomkun at 2026-03-18T12:45:38Z:
**Comment:**

This is a **well-defined, one-day task** — denylist support for exec-approvals.

- ✅ Clear use case
- ✅ Existing workarounds (300+ allowlist entries) prove the need
- ✅ Community discussion already shaped the design (argsMatch, ask/deny)
- ✅ Reference implementation exists (CRE plugin)

**Next step:** Someone pick this up and open a PR. It's not complex, just needs implementing.

Let's close this after a PR is merged. 🔧

- steipete at 2026-04-26T00:21:55Z:
Codex review: keeping this open for maintainer follow-up; there is still a little grit to resolve.

Keep this issue open. Current main still has no native exec-approvals denylist mode, denylist array, `ask: "on-match"`, or deny-pattern evaluation path; source, protocol schema, Control UI, tests, and docs all remain allowlist-focused. The SafeClaw and CRE links plus the closed implementation PRs are useful prior art, but they do not replace a merged native policy change, and the latest maintainer comment explicitly kept this open for follow-up.

Required change / next step:

This should stay in maintainer review because the next step is a security-sensitive policy design choice across core exec approvals, gateway/node protocol, CLI, UI, docs, and related tracker #41140, not a narrow safe automation patch.

Best possible solution:

Define an additive, version-safe native exec approvals policy that evaluates deny patterns before broad allow decisions, supports command and argument matching consistently across gateway and node exec paths, exposes configuration in CLI and Control UI, documents the security model, and either folds #41140 into this design or makes #41140 the canonical narrower tracker.

Acceptance criteria:

- pnpm test src/infra/exec-approvals-policy.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-config.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-allowlist.ts
- pnpm test src/gateway/node-invoke-system-run-approval.test.ts src/gateway/node-invoke-system-run-approval-match.test.ts
- pnpm test src/gateway/protocol/exec-approvals-validators.test.ts
- pnpm test ui/src/ui/views/config.browser.test.ts
- pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed files>

What I checked:

- **Policy enums exclude requested modes:** `ExecSecurity` is still `deny | allowlist | full`, and `ExecAsk` is still `off | on-miss | always`; there is no `denylist` security mode or `on-match` ask mode. ([`src/infra/exec-approvals.ts:22`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/542821cd1e60/src/infra/exec-approvals.ts#L22), [542821cd1e60](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/542821cd1e60))
- **Normalizer drops unsupported values:** `sanitizeExecApprovalPolicy` preserves only the three existing security values and three existing ask values, so proposed `denylist` / `on-match` fields are not accepted as policy values. ([`src/infra/exec-approvals.ts:346`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/542821cd1e60/src/infra/exec-approvals.ts#L346), [542821cd1e60](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/542821cd1e60))
- **Effective policy is allowlist-only:** `resolveExecApprovalsFromFile` merges wildcard and agent allowlist entries only, and `requiresExecApproval` prompts only for allowlist misses under `security: "allowlist"`; there is no deny-match branch before allow/full behavior. ([`src/infra/exec-approvals.ts:749`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/542821cd1e60/src/infra/exec-approvals.ts#L749), [542821cd1e60](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/542821cd1e60))
- **Gateway protocol has no denylist field:** `ExecApprovalsAgentSchema` exposes policy fields plus `allowlist` and uses `additionalProperties: false`, so gateway/node approval set payloads do not accept a `denylist` collection. ([`src/gateway/protocol/schema/exec-approvals.ts:29`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/542821cd1e60/src/gateway/protocol/schema/exec-approvals.ts#L29), [542821cd1e60](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/542821cd1e60))
- **Control UI only edits existing modes and allowlists:** The Nodes exec approvals view defines only `deny | allowlist | full` and `off | on-miss | always`, renders those option lists, and manages an Allowlist editor rather than a denylist editor. ([`ui/src/ui/views/nodes-exec-approvals.ts:15`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/542821cd1e60/ui/src/ui/views/nodes-exec-approvals.ts#L15), [542821cd1e60](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/542821cd1e60))
- **Docs document allowlist-only knobs:** The public exec approvals docs list only the existing security and ask modes and describe Control UI editing for defaults, per-agent overrides, and allowlists. Public docs: [`docs/tools/exec-approvals.md`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/exec-approvals). ([`docs/tools/exec-approvals.md:118`](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/exec-approvals), [542821cd1e60](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/542821cd1e60))

Users can only access their own `memory/{userId}/` directory - Shared memory files at `memory/` root are accessible to all ### Memory Tools - **memory_search** and **memory_get** tools updated - Accept optional `senderId` parameter - Falls back to `senderId` from inbound context when session key doesn't contain userId ### Plugin Integration - **extensions/memory-core/index.ts** updated - Passes `requesterSenderId` from plugin context to memory tools ## How It Works ``` Session Type | userId Source | Memory Path -------------------|------------------------|--------------------------- Direct Message | sessionKey extraction | memory/{userId}/YYYY-MM-DD.md Channel/Group | requesterSenderId | memory/{senderId}/YYYY-MM-DD.md Disabled (legacy) | none | memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md (shared) ``` ## Security - Users cannot access other users' memory directories - Path validation in `isMemoryPath()` prevents directory traversal - Shared memory at `memory/` root is accessible to all (for global context) ## Testing - Unit tests added in `src/memory/internal.test.ts` - All existing tests pass ## Breaking Changes None. The feature is opt-in via configuration and defaults to the new isolated behavior when `memory.isolation.enabled` is not set (defaults to `true`). Users who want the old shared behavior can set: ```json { "memory": { "isolation": { "enabled": false } } } ``` ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-15T12:08:00Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR adds two independent features: per-user memory isolation (storing memory files under `memory/{userId}/` per session) and a config backup/restore system with automatic gateway rollback on startup failure. The core memory isolation logic in `isMemoryPath`, `listMemoryFiles`, and the manager layer is well-implemented and well-tested. The config backup/restore infrastructure is similarly solid overall. **Issues found:** - **`createConfigBackup` bypasses `ioFs` abstraction** (`src/config/config-backup-restore.ts:149`): The backup file is written using the raw imported `fs` module instead of the injected `ioFs`, while the immediately following `ioFs.stat` call reads from the mock. This breaks any mock-based test of `createConfigBackup` and is inconsistent with every other I/O call in the function. - **Memory isolation silently disabled on QMD backend** (`src/memory/search-manager.ts:44-49`): The new `userId` parameter is never forwarded to `QmdMemoryManager.create`. Users on the QMD backend will see no isolation even when `memory.isolation.enabled` is `true`. - **`--last` CLI flag is a no-op** (`src/cli/config-cli.ts:538`): The `--last` option is defined, documented, and passed into `runConfigRestore`, but `opts.last` is never read in the function body. The code always silently uses the newest backup whenever `--backup-path` is omitted, regardless of whether `--last` was passed. - **Unescaped filename in regex** (`src/config/config-backup-restore.ts:89`): The config filename (`openclaw.json`) is interpolated directly into a `RegExp` without escaping, causing `.` to act as a wildcard and potentially match unintended files.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Needs fixes before merging — two logic bugs can silently break memory isolation and the backup testability contract. - The memory isolation feature works correctly for the builtin backend but silently fails for QMD users due to the missing `userId` forward. The `ioFs` bypass in `createConfigBackup` breaks the mock abstraction. The `--last` CLI flag does nothing. These are all concrete, reproducible bugs rather than edge-case concerns. - `src/memory/search-manager.ts` (QMD isolation gap) and `src/config/config-backup-restore.ts` (`fs` vs `ioFs` and regex escaping) need the most attention.

### Summary

### Steps to reproduce

Expected: ElevenLabs audio played back.

Actual: OpenAI voice plays despite ElevenLabs processing the request successfully.

```markdown
- Hollychou924 at 2026-03-22T11:20:52Z:
## Analysis: ElevenLabs generates audio but OpenAI voice plays — provider selection race or fallback silent override

The key diagnostic clue from the reporter:

> `openclaw status` shows **Provider: elevenlabs (configured)** but **Last attempt shows Provider: openai`**

This tells us ElevenLabs is correctly configured but the **runtime delivery path silently switched to OpenAI**. Two likely causes:

### Cause A — Silent fallback on ElevenLabs failure (most likely)

The TTS pipeline calls ElevenLabs successfully (confirmed by portal showing API calls), but something in the OpenClaw audio delivery path fails — e.g., the audio format returned by ElevenLabs isn't handled correctly, the file write path fails, or the playback step gets a null result. The pipeline then silently falls back to OpenAI.

**Diagnostic:** Check if `messages.tts.fallback` is configured (or defaults to `openai`). If so, any post-generation error triggers the fallback without surfacing the original error.

**Fix:** The fallback should log a warning when it activates — e.g.:
```
[tts] ElevenLabs generation succeeded but delivery failed (reason: <error>) — falling back to openai
```
Currently this transition is silent.

### Cause B — Provider selection logic reads stale/wrong provider at delivery time

The TTS system may select the provider at two different points:
1. At generation time (correctly reads `elevenlabs` from config)
2. At playback/delivery time (reads a different value — possibly the session's last-used provider)

If the delivery path has a separate provider lookup that isn't synced with the generation path, it can generate ElevenLabs audio and then discard it, playing OpenAI audio instead.

### What to check

```bash
# Check if fallback is silently triggering
grep -i 'tts\|elevenlabs\|fallback' ~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log | tail -50

# Check what audio file is actually being sent
# ElevenLabs returns MP3; OpenAI returns MP3 or OPUS
# The file format of what gets played would confirm which provider's output was used
```

### Fix

1. Make fallback activation visible: always log when fallback triggers and why
2. Audit TTS provider selection: ensure the same provider is used for both generation AND delivery, not read separately at each step
3. If ElevenLabs audio is generated successfully, it should always be used — fallback should only trigger if generation itself fails

- Artyomkun at 2026-03-22T22:39:37Z:
@Hollychou924 Great analysis — this is a silent fallback that's confusing users.

@steipete 

**Key diagnostic:**
- ElevenLabs API calls are logged on the provider side (so generation succeeded)
- But what gets played is OpenAI (so fallback triggered)

**Likely cause:**
Post‑generation step fails — maybe the audio format, file write, or playback path. Fallback kicks in without logging, so user only sees "ElevenLabs is configured, but I hear OpenAI."

**Fix suggestions:**
1. **Log fallback activation** — at least a warning with the reason
2. **Check format compatibility** — if ElevenLabs returns something OpenClaw can't play, convert or warn
3. **Single provider selection** — ensure the same provider is used for both generation and delivery, not read separately

**For debugging:**
- Compare the audio file that was actually sent (MP3 from ElevenLabs vs OpenAI)
- Check if `messages.tts.fallback` is set (or defaults to OpenAI)
- Look for any errors after "ElevenLabs generation succeeded" in logs

**Impact:**
This makes users think ElevenLabs is broken, even though it's working and billing. A clear warning would save them hours of config checking.

Thanks for the detailed analysis!

Body:
```markdown
# fix(models): strip remaining provider self prefixes

## Summary

- Fixes native model normalization for the remaining self-prefixed providers called out in #88770: Google variants, xAI, OpenAI, and Vercel AI Gateway.
- Adds a shared self-prefix strip helper and keeps existing provider-specific alias behavior intact.
- Extends regression coverage for provider catalog normalization and fallback candidate normalization so `provider/provider-model` shapes do not leak through the chain.

## Linked context

Closes #88770

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: self-prefixed native model ids were retained for several providers, producing doubled model refs such as `google/google/gemini-2.0-flash` in downstream lookup paths.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout at exact PR head `e5c6af5a462397cfa13e00711f7b55d8809bdea7`, using the Gemini API key from 1Password item `AI API Key - Google Gemini - GEMINI_API_KEY - steipete-m5` without printing the secret.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: tmux-backed `op item get ... --vault Molty` scoped to the known Gemini item, then `fnm exec --using=24.15.0 node_modules/.bin/tsx .tmp-pr88781-live-proof.ts`; the script imported `normalizeStaticProviderModelIdWithPolicies`, normalized `google/gemini-2.0-flash`, and called Google GenerateContent with the normalized model id.
- Evidence after fix: terminal output from the live run:

```text
pr88781_live_model_proof_begin
head=e5c6af5a462397cfa13e00711f7b55d8809bdea7
provider=google
configuredModel=google/gemini-2.0-flash
normalizedModel=gemini-2.0-flash
doubledPrefix=absent
secretSource=1password:AI API Key - Google Gemini - GEMINI_API_KEY - steipete-m5
googleGenerateContentStatus=200
googleGenerateContentOk=true
responseTextRedacted=len:2;containsOk:true
pr88781_live_model_proof_end
pr88781_live_exit:0
```

- Observed result after fix: the self-prefixed Google model ref normalized to the provider-local id, the doubled prefix was absent, and the real Google API accepted that normalized model id with HTTP 200.
- What was not tested: live calls for xAI, OpenAI, and Vercel AI Gateway; those provider branches are covered by the same normalization helper and focused regression tests.
- Before evidence: before this patch, the same helper retained `google/gemini-2.0-flash`, `xai/grok-4-fast`, `openai/gpt-5.4`, and `vercel-ai-gateway/opus-4.6`, allowing downstream `provider/provider/...` lookup shapes.

## Tests and validation

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr88781-vitest-cache2 fnm exec --using=24.15.0 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run packages/model-catalog-core/src/provider-model-id-normalization.test.ts src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts` passed: 2 Vitest shards, 79 tests.
- `fnm exec --using=24.15.0 node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.packages.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile /tmp/pr88781-rebased-test-packages.tsbuildinfo` passed.
- `fnm exec --using=24.15.0 node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile /tmp/pr88781-rebased-test-src.tsbuildinfo` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? Yes.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No.
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No.
- Highest-risk area: model id normalization for provider-prefixed values.
- Mitigation: the patch only strips a prefix matching the current provider branch, leaves gateway/cross-provider model refs alone, and adds focused regression coverage.

## Current review state

Ready for maintainer review. Nothing is waiting on the author beyond CI/reviewer feedback.

Labels: agents, size: S, proof: supplied, P1, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 auth-provider, status: 👀 ready for maintainer look

- State: OPEN

- Labels: size: XS, extensions: acpx, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient

Body:
```markdown
test(acpx): mirror resolver's path construction in MCP server test helper

The ACPx config test expected source-mode (tsx loader) paths for built-in MCP servers, but the resolver correctly prefers dist entries when they exist. Update the test helper to match this runtime behavior.

- Mirror resolver's path construction: compute openClawRoot from resolveAcpxPluginRoot, then use path.join/path.relative to build the correct absolute dist path.
- Remove the broken entrypoint.replace("/src/", "/dist/") which never matched because the test passes "src/mcp/..." without a leading separator.

Fixes #80431

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** The test helper `expectedMcpServerArgs` used `entrypoint.replace("/src/", "/dist/")` which never matched because the caller passes "src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.ts" (no leading `/`). This caused the dist-existence check to silently check the wrong path (`src/mcp/*.js` instead of `dist/mcp/*.js`) and always fall back to source-mode args, even when `dist/` existed.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Ubuntu 22.04, Node 20, pnpm 9. Built dist exists for built-in MCP servers under `dist/mcp/`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```
cd /tmp/openclaw-fork
git checkout fix/issue-80431-acpx-dist-path
pnpm build
ls -la dist/mcp/  # confirm dist exists
npx vitest run extensions/acpx/src/config.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix:**
Terminal output showing the corrected test helper now constructs the correct absolute dist path:
```
$ npx vitest run extensions/acpx/src/config.test.ts
 PASS  extensions/acpx/src/config.test.ts
   ✓ injects the built-in plugin-tools MCP server only when explicitly enabled
   ✓ injects the built-in openclaw-tools MCP server only when explicitly enabled
```

Before the fix, the helper computed `distEntry` as `src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.js` (because `/src/` did not match) and `fs.existsSync` returned false, so the test always fell back to source-mode args even when `dist/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.js` existed.

**Observed result after fix:** The helper now computes the same absolute dist path as the production resolver (`resolvePluginToolsMcpServerConfig`), correctly returning `[distEntry]` when `dist/` is present and falling back to `["--import", tsx, sourceEntry]` otherwise.

**What was not tested:** No runtime resolver logic changed; only the test helper was updated to mirror the resolver's path construction.
```

GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 70002 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/70002 - Title: ci: skip docs sync & translate-trigger workflows in forks - State: OPEN - Author: xudaiyanzi - Labels: size: XS, triage: risky-infra, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, proof: sufficient, P2, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, merge-risk: 🚨 automation, status: 👀 ready for maintainer look, proof: 📸 screenshot Body: ```markdown ## Summary - **Problem:** Two upstream-only workflows (`docs-sync-publish.yml`, `docs-translate-trigger-release.yml`) fail with `Authentication failed for 'https://github.com/openclaw/docs.git/'` on every push to `main` in any fork, because they rely on the `OPENCLAW_DOCS_SYNC_TOKEN` secret that only exists in `openclaw/openclaw`. - **Why it matters:** Creates a red ✗ on every normal fork-sync (`git fetch upstream main && git push origin main`), confuses new contributors, and spams fork Actions dashboards with irrecoverable failures. - **What changed:** Added `if: github.repository == 'openclaw/openclaw'` to the single job in each of the two workflows — the same guard pattern already used ~30 times across the repo (`ci.yml`, `codeql.yml`, `control-ui-locale-refresh.yml`). - **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to workflow logic, secrets, the publish repo, the sync script (`scripts/docs-sync-publish.mjs`), documentation, or any other workflow. Behavior in `openclaw/openclaw` is byte-for-byte identical. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause (if applicable) - **Root cause:** The two workflows were added without the fork-guard expression that the rest of the CI already uses. When the workflow runs in a fork, `${{ secrets.OPENCLAW_DOCS_SYNC_TOKEN }}` expands to an empty string, so the clone/push URL becomes `https://x-access-token:@github.com/openclaw/docs.git/` and GitHub rejects it with `Invalid username or token. Password authentication is not supported for Git operations.` - **Missing detection / guardrail:** No `if: github.repository == 'openclaw/openclaw'` on the job, even though the same repository relies on that exact check in ~30 other places to keep fork runs safe. - **Contributing context:** The workflows were likely adapted from local-only automation; the push-to-external-repo step is what makes the fork-guard a requirement rather than a nicety. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient - **Target test or file:** N/A — GitHub Actions `if:` expressions are evaluated by GitHub's runner and are not covered by the project's test suites. The guard syntax itself is validated by GitHub's workflow parser. - **Scenario the test should lock in:** On fork `main` push, the two docs workflows must evaluate to Skipped rather than running. - **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** A job-level `if:` is the idiomatic, zero-cost mechanism GitHub Actions provides for this; the repo already uses it extensively for the same reason. - **Existing test that already covers this (if any):** N/A. - **If no new test is added, why not:** No project-level test harness exists for workflow fork-safety; the guard is the test. Empirical verification via `workflow_dispatch` on this PR branch in a fork (see Human Verification). ## User-visible / Behavior Changes None. End users, plugins, gateway, UI, API, and config are unaffected. Only the fork Actions dashboard changes: the two workflows report **Skipped** (grey) instead of **Failed** (red). ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before (fork push to main): push main -> docs-sync-publish job runs -> git clone/push openclaw/docs -> token="" -> Authentication failed -> 5 retries -> exit 1 (red ✗) After (fork push to main): push main -> docs-sync-publish job evaluated -> github.repository != 'openclaw/openclaw' -> Skipped (grey, no secret access, no network call) ``` Upstream behavior (openclaw/openclaw:main): unchanged. ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-04-22T05:50:52Z:

This prevents authentication failures in forks where `OPENCLAW_DOCS_SYNC_TOKEN` is unavailable, with zero behavior change on the upstream repository.

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #63760: Announce runs for subagent/ACP completions waste tokens on cold cache with parent model
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 63760
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/63760
- Title: Announce runs for subagent/ACP completions waste tokens on cold cache with parent model
- State: OPEN
- Author: liu51115
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:auth-provider, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Problem

When a subagent or ACP session (e.g. Claude Code) completes, OpenClaw runs an "announce" embedded run to deliver the result to the parent agent. This announce run:

1. **Uses the parent agent's model** — e.g. Opus at $25/MTok output for what is essentially a message relay
2. **Starts with a cold cache** — loads the full agent context (system prompt, conversation history, pinned files) fresh, paying full cache-write costs
3. **Does minimal work** — rewrites "CC finished, here is the result" in the agent's voice

### Cost impact

For an Opus agent with ~80k context:
- Cache write: ~80k × $6.25/MTok ≈ $0.50
- Output: ~200 tokens × $25/MTok ≈ $0.005
- **Total: ~$0.50+ per announcement**, for a message that could be delivered for ~$0.03 on Sonnet

With frequent CC spawns (10-20/day), this adds up to $5-10/day in announce overhead alone.

## Proposed solutions

1. **`announce.model` config** — allow overriding the model used for announce embedded runs (e.g. use Sonnet instead of parent model)
2. **`announce.lightContext`** — strip conversation history from announce runs, only include the completion result + minimal system prompt
3. **Both** — light context + cheaper model would reduce announce cost by ~95%

## Current workaround

Setting `delivery: { mode: "none" }` on spawns and manually checking report files. Works but loses the automatic notification flow.

## Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.4.9
- Parent agent: Opus 4.6 (Anthropic direct)
- ACP backend: Claude Code
```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #84754: [Bug]: WebChat typing indicator persists after assistant response completes
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 84754
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/84754
- Title: [Bug]: WebChat typing indicator persists after assistant response completes
- State: CLOSED
- Author: qq517995904
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:needs-live-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🐚 platinum hermit

Body:
```markdown
## Description

After the assistant finishes sending a reply in WebChat, the "typing indicator" (three dots / status bar) continues to show as if the assistant is still generating, even though the response has been fully delivered. This causes confusion and misleading UI state.

## Environment

- **OpenClaw version:** 2026.5.18 → 2026.5.19 (persists across both versions)
- **Channel:** WebChat (control UI)
- **Setup:** macOS, local gateway (loopback)
- **Proxy:** nil (direct loopback to gateway)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open WebChat (Web UI at gateway port)
2. Send any message to the assistant
3. Wait for the assistant to fully respond (message appears complete)
4. Observe the typing indicator at the bottom of the conversation

## Expected Behavior

The typing indicator should stop and disappear **immediately** after the assistant's response text is fully delivered.

## Actual Behavior

The typing indicator continues to show for an extended time after the response has been fully displayed, or never clears until the page is refreshed. This looks like the indicator state is not properly being reset on the frontend after the message stream ends.

## Notes

- This was first noticed on 2026.5.18 and persists through the 2026.5.19 update.
- Refreshing the page (⌘+R) resets the indicator.
- This appears to be a WebChat frontend rendering issue rather than a server-side problem.
- Related existing issue: #67035 (Windows chat UI typing indicator regression, but different platform)
```

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

`sessions_spawn` accepts arbitrary, unknown `agentId` values and silently instantiates a new "subagent" using default model + empty bootstrap when the requester's `subagents.allowAgents` contains the `*` wildcard. There is no registry validation: the wildcard skips the check entirely instead of meaning "any *registered* agent". This is undocumented behavior with side effects.

## Discovery

While auditing a peer-agent boundary violation, we noticed `sessions_spawn` could target an arbitrary peer agent name (e.g., `graybeard`) even though that agent doesn't exist in the requester's agent registry — only peer-agent gateways host it. Two probes confirmed:

```jsonc
// Probe 1
sessions_spawn({ agentId: "nopagent", task: "...", mode: "run" })
// → status: "accepted", childSessionKey: "agent:nopagent:subagent:..."

// Probe 2
sessions_spawn({ agentId: "bogus_test_agent_xyz", task: "diagnostic..." })
// → status: "accepted", model: "claude-opus-4-7" (defaults), no agent config
```

The bogus session ran successfully and replied: *"I am the bogus_test_agent_xyz subagent, running on the anthropic/claude-opus-4-7 model."*

Both child workspaces were materialized on disk: `~/.openclaw/agents/nopagent/` and `~/.openclaw/agents/bogus_test_agent_xyz/`.

## Root Cause

`src/agents/subagent-target-policy.ts` short-circuits to `ok: true` when `allowAgents` contains `*`:

```ts
// resolveSubagentTargetPolicy
const allowed = resolveSubagentAllowedTargetIds({ requesterAgentId, allowAgents });
if (allowed.allowAny || allowed.allowedIds.includes(targetAgentId)) {
  return { ok: true };
}
```

`resolveSubagentAllowedTargetIds` does populate `configuredIds` from `params.configuredAgentIds`, but those ids are only used to build the *display* allowed-list — the validation path never consults the registry when `allowAny` is true:

```ts
if (policy.allowAny) {
  const configuredIds = (params.configuredAgentIds ?? [])
    .map((id) => normalizeAgentId(id))
    .filter(Boolean);
  return {
    allowAny: true,
    allowedIds: Array.from(new Set(configuredIds)).toSorted((a, b) => a.localeCompare(b)),
  };
}
```

So `*` literally means "accept any string as a valid agent id and provision a new agent on demand using defaults." There is no callsite that checks `targetAgentId ∈ configuredAgentIds`.

## Behavior When agentId Is Unknown

1. Spawn gate accepts the arbitrary id (no error, no warning).
2. Skip agent registry lookup entirely.
3. Apply `agents.defaults.model.primary` (no per-agent model config).
4. Apply default/empty bootstrap (no per-agent `SOUL.md`, no DB-backed bootstrap rows, no domain context).
5. Create a fresh agent home directory at `~/.openclaw/agents/<arbitrary_string>/` with subdirs (`agent/`, `sessions/`).
6. Spawn it as a subagent parented to the requester. Full tool access (workspace, exec, etc.).
7. Persist transcripts under the bogus identity until manually cleaned.

The resulting agent has the requester's workspace and tool surface but inherits no policy or identity guardrails specific to the spoofed name.

## Impact

### Security / scoping
- Any caller with `sessions_spawn` and `allowAgents: ["*"]` can name-collide or impersonate any agent string — including peer-agent names from other gateways (e.g., `newhart`, `graybeard`). The spawn doesn't actually reach those peers (they're separate gateways), but the in-gateway clone runs with the requester's permissions while *looking like* the peer in logs, transcripts, and channel announces.
- A simple typo (`coderr` for `coder`) produces a silent fallback to a default-configured agent instead of an error, which can mask real configuration drift.
- The `*` wildcard's intuitive meaning ("any registered agent in my allowlist") is wildly different from its actual meaning ("auto-provision any string"). Operators are likely to assume the safer semantic.

### Filesystem hygiene
`~/.openclaw/agents/` accumulates directories for every bogus, typo'd, deprecated, or accidentally-spawned name and never garbage-collects them. Sample from a long-running production gateway:

```
argus-security/   claude/         claude-code/    default/
docs-agent/       erato/          gemini-cli/     git-agent/
graybeard/        iris-artist/    librarian-agent/ main/
newhart/          nhr-agent/      qa-agent/       ...
```

Most of these have no corresponding entries in `agents.json` and represent historical accidental spawns or deprecated agent names. Each has its own `sessions/*.jsonl` transcripts. Over time this becomes a sprawl problem and an audit headache.

### Observability
- `agents_list` correctly shows only registered agents, so operators auditing their roster won't see the rogue identities even though they exist on disk.
- Transcripts under bogus identities aren't surfaced anywhere unless you list the filesystem directly.

## Proposed Fix

**Primary (default behavior):** Validate target agent id against the configured registry even when `allowAgents: ["*"]` is set. Replace the wildcard semantic with *"any agent present in `agents.list`"*.

In `resolveSubagentTargetPolicy`, when `allowAny` is true, still require:

```ts
if (allowed.allowAny) {
  if (allowed.allowedIds.includes(targetAgentId)) return { ok: true };
  return {
    ok: false,
    allowedText: allowed.allowedIds.join(", ") || "none",
    error: `agentId "${targetAgentId}" is not in the configured agent registry`,
  };
}
```

(Make sure `configuredAgentIds` is actually threaded into `resolveSubagentAllowedTargetIds` at all `resolveSubagentTargetPolicy` callsites — right now it's accepted but the policy resolver doesn't pass it through.)

**Opt-out flag:** Add `agents.subagents.strictRegistry: boolean` (default `true`) to let operators preserve the current auto-provision behavior if they've built on top of it. When `false`, restore the pre-fix lax behavior with a one-time deprecation warning logged at spawn time.

Suggested config schema:

```ts
agents: {
  subagents: {
    strictRegistry?: boolean; // default true
    // existing fields...
  }
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- `sessions_spawn({ agentId: "<unknown>" })` returns an error like `agentId "<unknown>" is not in the configured agent registry (allowed: a, b, c)` instead of accepting and provisioning.
- No filesystem artifacts (no new `~/.openclaw/agents/<unknown>/` directory, no transcript file).
- Existing tests for `subagent-target-policy` updated; new test covers the `*` + unknown-id case.
- Setting `agents.subagents.strictRegistry: false` restores current behavior (auto-provision) for backward compat, with a `WARN` log per spawn.
- Documentation update in `docs/concepts/session-tool.md` (or `multi-agent.md`) explicitly stating the registry validation contract and the opt-out flag.

## Discovery Context (for changelog)

Found 2026-05-19 by NOVA agent (production) during a peer-agent boundary audit. The trigger was noticing that `sessions_spawn(agentId: "graybeard")` had succeeded earlier in the day even though Graybeard is a peer agent running in a separate gateway. The follow-up probes with synthetic names (`nopagent`, `bogus_test_agent_xyz`) confirmed there is no registry check on the `*` path.

## Related

- `src/agents/subagent-target-policy.ts` — primary bug location
- `src/agents/subagent-target-policy.test.ts` — needs coverage for unknown-id case
- `docs/concepts/session-tool.md` — needs registry-validation contract documented
- `docs/concepts/multi-agent.md` — could note the strictRegistry knob

```

Fixes #90088.

Adds the missing native Anthropic API-key static catalog entry for Claude Haiku 4.5 and maps rolling/dotted Haiku 4.5 refs to the dated Anthropic API id.

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Fixes a Codex app-server startup/reporting crash when bootstrap context files are supplied with `path` and `content` but no `name`.

The patch keeps path-based lookup first, treats the filename as optional, and derives the report display name from the path when needed.

## Why

OpenClaw can carry context files shaped like:

```json
{ "path": "/workspace/AGENTS.md", "content": "..." }
```

The Codex system prompt report path later assumed `name` was always present and could call `.trim()` through the basename helper with `undefined`.

## What changed

- Guard optional bootstrap file names in the Codex injection stats path.
- Keep lookup by full `path` before trying filename or basename lookup.
- Derive a stable display name from `path` when `name` is absent.
- Add a regression test through `runCodexAppServerAttempt()` with a hook-supplied path-only bootstrap file.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Codex app-server startup/reporting could crash while building the system prompt report when a bootstrap context file had `path` and `content` but no `name`; the failing path was `runCodexAppServerAttempt -> buildCodexSystemPromptReport -> buildCodexBootstrapInjectionStats -> readCodexIndexedContextFileContent -> getCodexContextFileBasename`.

Real environment tested: isolated Linux OpenClaw testserver clone of `openclaw/openclaw`, Node v22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.0. This was a standalone `node --import tsx` OpenClaw run outside Vitest. It used a provider-free Codex JSON-RPC stdio app-server process, no live provider credentials, no OAuth, and no secrets.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: applied the patch to the isolated OpenClaw checkout, then ran the standalone proof runner:
